EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trained spotters in Tavernier reported that strong south winds blew water completely out of canals on the Florida Bay side, causing numerous moored vessels to rest on the bay bottom.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown over onto Harry Harris Drive in Tavernier, damaging power and water lines.  Limbs from the top of the tree caused minor damage to the roofing of two homes.

The Olive Wildfire in western Putnam county was burning since Nov. 24, 2011, north of State Road 20 just west of Interlachen and east of Melrose. As of Jan. 9th, the fire had burned about 1000 acres in the Levy Prairie. Smoke from the fire combined with fog to produce near zero visibilities at times. Several roads were closed in the area on the 8th and 9th, including portions of State Road 20 between County Roads 315 and 20A. Ochwilla Elementary had to be closed on the 9th due to smoke in the area, and opened late on the 10th due to smoke. Several auto accidents were blamed on poor visibilities in the vicinity of the fire on the 8th and the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Olive wildfire continued to burn through January 2012, with most impacts reported on the 8th-10th. Smoke caused road closures, auto accidents, and the closing of a school.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Debby moved across the area from the northeast Gulf of Mexico. Deep tropical moisture combined with a stalled frontal boundary across north Florida over a period of several days caused extensive, flooding rainfall, as well as historic river flooding on the St. Marys River. A few severe storms developed each day, but the main impact was flooding rainfall and extensive river flooding which flooded homes in Baker, Charlton, Camden and Nassau counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 6:30 pm, reports of widespread flooding across the Jacksonville Westside and portions of northern Clay county poured into the NWS office. At 6:30 pm, the public reported a part of a yard under water and water rising quickly due to a clogged drain about 5 miles WSW of downtown Jacksonville on the Westside. At 6:50 pm, flood waters entered a home on 105th Street between Blanding Blvd and Jammes Road on the Westside. At 7 pm, the public reported water in a home on West 22nd Street. At 7:10 pm, the public reported 1-2 feet of standing water at the intersection of Port Street and Melba Street. Also at 7:10 pm, the public reported flood waters in 2 homes on Antoinette Lane on the Jacksonville Westside. At 7:20 pm, the public reported a car was stranded due to flooding on Rensselaer Road. At 7:30 pm, the public reported standing water about 1 to 2 feet deep over Betsy Drive. At 7:35 pm, the public reported 2-3 feet of standing water over Columbus Ave. The water was overflowing into yards. At 9:02 pm, the public reported water was over the road on the 3600 block of Post Street and was lapping at the doors of homes. The cost of damage was estimated, and is likely under-estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Debby moved across the area from the northeast Gulf of Mexico. Deep tropical moisture combined with a stalled frontal boundary across north Florida over a period of several days caused extensive, flooding rainfall, as well as historic river flooding on the St. Marys River. A few severe storms developed each day, but the main impact was flooding rainfall and extensive river flooding which flooded homes in Baker, Charlton, Camden and Nassau counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 10 pm, the public reported flooding on Sawpit Road on Black Hammock Island. At 1010  pm, the public reported flooding into a home on Rose Street, about 5 miles south of the Jacksonville International Airport. At 10:42 pm, the public reported flooding over Catoma Street in Orange Park. Water was over the street and up to doors of houses. Residents evacuated. The cost of damage was estimated, and it is likely an under-estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lack of rainfall during the dry season led to the onset of severe drought conditions over interior sections of southwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The La Ni��a pattern which has prevailed through the South Florida dry season contributed to the onset of severe drought conditions over interior southwest Florida. Rainfall amounts over the area from November 1, 2011 through the end of February 2012 were only in the 4 to 5 inch range, which is about half of the normal rain percent of normal for that time period. Pasture conditions ranged from poor to fair across the area. The level of Fisheating Creek was around 1.5 feet which was about 1 foot below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough of low pressure moved through the area, bringing with it a pre-frontal squall line.  Although wind fields were strong, limited instability precluded a more widespread event.  Scattered pockets of damaging winds occurred, and these were limited to tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near Bonnett Pond Rd.  Monetary damage was estimated.

A cold front moved southeast across the Florida Peninsula through the evening of January 12th and the morning of January 13th, with Canadian high pressure building into the area behind the front. This allowed for breezy northerly winds and cold air advection to bring temperatures down to well below freezing.  Although the coldest temperatures occurred on January 15th, much of the Nature Coast experienced several hours of freezing temperatures on the morning of January 16th, which at some sites was the third consecutive morning with low temperatures below freezing.  No crop damage was reported.||Levy County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for around 7 hours across parts of the county.  The coldest temperature reported was 28 degrees at the RAWS station at Lower Suwannee.||Citrus County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for around 2 hours across much of the county.  The coldest temperature reported was 30 degrees at the AWOS site at Crystal River.||Hernando County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for around 2 to 4 hours across much of the county.  The coldest temperature reported was 29 degrees at the FAWN site at Brooksville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Levy County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for around 7 hours across parts of the county.  The coldest temperature reported was 28 degrees at the RAWS station at Lower Suwannee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Debby moved across the area from the northeast Gulf of Mexico. Deep tropical moisture combined with a stalled frontal boundary across north Florida over a period of several days caused extensive, flooding rainfall, as well as historic river flooding on the St. Marys River. A few severe storms developed each day, but the main impact was flooding rainfall and extensive river flooding which flooded homes in Baker, Charlton, Camden and Nassau counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe flash flooding occurred in White Springs with roads washed out to a depth of 8 to 12 feet. The first roadway washed out was 1st Street which was completely severed. The water then flowed along a stream bed to Jewett Street which was also completely severed to a depth of 12 ft. One culvert pipe was completely washed away and was not located. Kendrick Street was then flooded and approximately 20 ft of road bed was either washed away or undermined. The cost of damage was estimated, and it is likely grossly under-estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front sagged slowly over north Florida during the day. This feature combined with a moist and unstable airmass triggered isolated storms near sea breeze and outflow mergers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was snapped due to thunderstorm winds. The tree fell on 2 cars, both of which were heavily damage by the tree and heavy rainfall. The cost of damage to the cars was estimated for inclusion of the event in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwesterly winds ahead of an approaching cold front brought in a surge of moisture that fueled early evening thunderstorms. A lightning strike from one of these storms struck and damaged a house in Land O Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house in Land O Lakes, damaging the fuse box, as well as shattering a couple of windows.  Damage was estimated as $1000 for 2 new windows and a new fuse box.

A surface front lingered near the Florida-Georgia state line. During the afternoon and evening, scattered severe thunderstorms developed over northeast Florida. Locally heavy rainfall caused flash flooding in Duval county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding occurred across east Jacksonville. At 5:27 pm, local broacast media reported Central Parkway on the Southside was under several feet of water. At 6:01 pm, a NWS employee reported Debutante Road was flooded by 1.5 ft of water. Several cars were stranded in the flood waters. A spotter on Debutante Road measured 14 inches of rainfall within 24 hrs at 6:01 pm that evening. At 6:26 pm, the public reported 1 to 2 feet of standing water on Indies Drive. At 7:42 pm that evening, the broadcast media reported 9.5 inches of rainfall that day near Kernan Blvd and Beach Blvd.  The cost of property damage was estimated for inclusion of the event in Storm Data.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer reported 7.5 inches of rainfall on Fleming Island since 12:30 pm that afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Debby moved across the area from the northeast Gulf of Mexico. Deep tropical moisture combined with a stalled frontal boundary across north Florida over a period of several days caused extensive, flooding rainfall, as well as historic river flooding on the St. Marys River. A few severe storms developed each day, but the main impact was flooding rainfall and extensive river flooding which flooded homes in Baker, Charlton, Camden and Nassau counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 6:45 pm, an NWS employee reported 1 foot of standing water over the I-295 and Blanding Blvd exit in Orange Park. At 6:46 pm, the public reported street flooding near Whitey's Fish Camp, about 2 feet deep. At 7:08 pm, the public reported around 1 foot of standing water near Blanding Blvd and Tanglewood in the Lakeside area. At 7:15 pm, the public reported over a foot of standing water at Timbermill Lane and Timberlake Drive. Water was over-flowing out of the storm drain. At 8:20 pm, the public reported flood water coming up to home porches near Bradford and Old Kings Road on the Jacksonville Southside. At 8:30 pm, the public reported a retention pond overflowed and water was almost inside a home. At 9:26 pm, the public reported major flooding at an apartment complex near San Jose and Hartley Road with water about 4 to 5 inches deep inside the apartments. The resident had to be rescued. At 9:45 pm, the public reported knee to waist deep water on the 3100 block of Stonebriar Ridge Road in Oakleaf Plantation. The cost of damage was estimated, and it is likely an under-estimate.

A surface front lingered near the Florida-Georgia state line. During the afternoon and evening, scattered severe thunderstorms developed over northeast Florida. Locally heavy rainfall caused flash flooding in Duval county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An employee measured 7.31 inches of rainfall within 24 hours. Ditches were near bank-full and there was minor street flooding in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Debby moved across the area from the northeast Gulf of Mexico. Deep tropical moisture combined with a stalled frontal boundary across north Florida over a period of several days caused extensive, flooding rainfall, as well as historic river flooding on the St. Marys River. A few severe storms developed each day, but the main impact was flooding rainfall and extensive river flooding which flooded homes in Baker, Charlton, Camden and Nassau counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 8:50 am on the 24th, a spotter reported flooded streets in Belleview. At 9 am, a spotter measured 4.5 inches of rainfall in Fairfield since midnight that morning. At 12:06 am on the 25th, a spotter measured 7.92 inches of rainfall on June 24th (a 24 hours total) about 6 miles south of the Ocala Airport. On the 25th at 10:21 am, a newspaper in Marion county reported State Road 40 in Ocala was closed due to high water. At 1:20 pm, the pubilc reported 10.25 inches near the Fellowship area in NW Marion county during the past 24 hours. At 5:15 pm that afternoon, a CoCoRaHS observer about 3 miles SSW of Micanopy reported 1.10 inches of rainfall in 30 minutes. Marion county had extensive problems with sinkholes, with an estimated 52 due to heavy rainfall from TS Debby. The cost of damage was estimated, and is likely a large under-estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Debby moved across the area from the northeast Gulf of Mexico. Deep tropical moisture combined with a stalled frontal boundary across north Florida over a period of several days caused extensive, flooding rainfall, as well as historic river flooding on the St. Marys River. A few severe storms developed each day, but the main impact was flooding rainfall and extensive river flooding which flooded homes in Baker, Charlton, Camden and Nassau counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm force wind gusts started to impact Duval county on 6/24 at 3:33 pm in tropical squalls. At 3:33 pm, a mesonet site at Jacksonville Beach measured a wind gust to 46 mph from the south with a sustained speed of 30 mph. At 3:41 pm, the Naval Station Mayport measured a gust to 44 mph. At 2:09 am on 6/25, the Mayport Naval Station ASOS measured a gust to 44 mph. At 2:36 am, that same station measured a gust to 45 mph. At 6:57 am, the ASOS at Naval Air Station Jacksonville measured a wind gust to 51 mph.

A few severe storms developed each day, but the main impact was flooding rainfall and extensive river flooding which flooded homes in Baker, Charlton, Camden and Nassau counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 9:15 pm on 6/24, strong wind gusts blew down trees and power lines in Flagler Beach. Emergency Management reported a power line blown down on South Central Avenue in Flagler Beach. At 9:32 pm, emergency management reported a tree down on power lines on Ramblewood Drive in Palm Coast. On 6/27 at 12:09 am, a spotter in west Palm Coast reported 7.20 inches of rainfall from 8 am to midnight.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding impacted much of Suwannee County. On 6/25 at 7:30 pm, law enforcement reported flooding at U.S. Highway 90 at County Road 137 in Wellborn. The intersection was closed due to deep water. At 7:30 pm, the public reported 8.5 inches of water accumulation in Live Oak. At the same time, a trained spotter reported reported streets were beginning to flood due to ditches over-flowing toward the roadway in Live Oak. At 8:02 pm, law enforcement reported a tree was blown down in the right lane of Interstate 10 near mile marker 292 due to saturated grounds and gusty winds. On 6/28 at 10:23 am, an NWS Storm  Survey reported a sinkhole 1 block from the Intersection of Highway 90 and State Road 129. A few structures were in danger of being engulfed into the expanding sinkhole.  The cost of damage was estimated and is likely grossly under-estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Active southwest steering flow continued a moist and unstable airmass over the area. These convective ingredients combined with lingering outflow boundaries from previous convection and the sea breezes brought scattered strong to severe storms to the area during the afternoon and evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved through South Florida on the night of January 2nd, followed by the coldest air of the winter season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped to below freezing for up to 10 hours over parts of Glades County on the night of January 3rd and morning of January 4th, and again for four hours on the morning of January 5th. Damage to crops mostly to vegetables with little impact on citrus. Damage total included in Hendry County summary.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped to below freezing for up to 9 hours over parts of Hendry County on the night of January 3rd and morning of January 4th, and again for two to four hours on the morning of January 5th. Damage to crops mostly to vegetables with little impact on citrus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved through South Florida on the night of January 2nd, followed by the coldest air of the winter season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped to below freezing for 8 to 10 hours over parts of western Palm Beach County on the night of January 3rd and morning of January 4th. Damage to crops mostly to vegetables with little impact on citrus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front over central Florida lifted northward as a warm front in advance of an approaching Gulf of Mexico Low pressure system. Locally heavy rainfall developed in the afternoon and evening over a moist and unstable airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reba Street was flooded near Philips Highway and the street was closed. Water was waist deep and property was flooded. The time of the causative event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mean mid and upper level troughing continued over the region with a moist and unstable airmass in place. An outflow boundary from convection over AL and GA moved over southeast GA during the afternoon, and provided a focus for strong to severe storms as it interacted with an unstable environment over northeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tire store, made of a steel building, had its roof blown off due to a down burst wind entering the structure through 3 of its open bay doors. All debris was outspread in a uniform direction. A plant nursery across the street had uniform damage as well. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front sagged slowly over north Florida during the day. This feature combined with a moist and unstable airmass triggered isolated storms near sea breeze and outflow mergers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported on State Road 21 northeast of Hawthorne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved through South Florida on the night of January 2nd, followed by the coldest air of the winter season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped to the freezing mark over parts of inland Miami-Dade County on the night of January 3rd and early morning of January 4th, with temperatures at these values for 2-4 hours. Areas most affected were the Redland and Homestead areas. Damage to most sensitive crops (beans, herbs, squash, asian vegetables) was about 15-20%. A few wind-protected fields suffered near-total losses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving pre-frontal squall line with isolated thunderstorms moved across northeast Florida during the morning. Instability was limited, but a strong low level jet of 50-60 knots between 1-1.5 kft produced isolated wind damage in thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 50 trees and power lines were blown down at the intersection of Dairy Road and Mangham Way, southwest of Lake City. A tree limb was blown down and caused minor damage to a home near the above intersection. The cost of damage was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front sagged slowly over north Florida during the day. This feature combined with a moist and unstable airmass triggered isolated storms near sea breeze and outflow mergers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell down after being struck by lightning in Bulow. The coat of damage was estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Highway 20 at Gordon Chapel Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front positioned over northeast Florida during the afternoon. Combined with daytime heating and passing upper level short wave trough energy, a few severe storms developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The general public measured 2.49 inches of rain in 45 minutes near Fort White.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front positioned over northeast Florida during the afternoon. Combined with daytime heating and passing upper level short wave trough energy, a few severe storms developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter measured 2.4 inches of rain in one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted northward over northeast Florida during the pre-dawn hours. Elevated instability combined with strong low level winds enhanced a storm that produced severe wind damage over Alachua county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees and large tree limbs were blown down near the intersection of Williston and Wacahoota Roads in southern Gainesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Debby moved across the area from the northeast Gulf of Mexico. Deep tropical moisture combined with a stalled frontal boundary across north Florida over a period of several days caused extensive, flooding rainfall, as well as historic river flooding on the St. Marys River. A few severe storms developed each day, but the main impact was flooding rainfall and extensive river flooding which flooded homes in Baker, Charlton, Camden and Nassau counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter at 2:30 am reported Anvers Road, amongst many across the Jacksonville Westside were flooded and impassable. At 3 am, an emergency manager reported of Suwannee county reported widespread roads across Live Oak as well as other more rural areas had severe flooding. Northern portions of Live Oak were evacuated due to flood waters. At 5 am, an NWS Storm Survey report observed severe flash flooding in White Springs of Hamilton county. Roads were washed out to a depth of 8 to 12 feet. The first road washed out was 1st Street in White Springs which was completely severed. The water then flowed along a stream bed to Jewett Street which was also completely severed to a depth of 12 feet. One culvert pipe was completely washed away and was not recovered. Kendrick Street was then flooded and approximately 20 feet of road bed was washed away or undermined. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across the western Florida Panhandle during the afternoon producing high winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters were reported to be three feet deep in the 2400 Block of Southwest 31st Place near the Phoenix Apartments with vehicles stranded in the floodwaters.  other flooding was reported around the county with sinkholes also opening on Tuesday evening.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of homes were under water about 12 miles south of the Suwannee River. The cost of damaged was estimated and is likely underestimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved east across the Florida Peninsula on the morning of January 27th, followed by a reinforcing cold front and high pressure over the following days.  This allowed temperatures over the Nature Coast to cool to below freezing for a few hours on the morning of January 30th.  No crop damage was reported.||Temperatures in parts of Levy County fell to below freezing for around 3 hours.  The coldest temperature reported was 29 degrees at the COOP station at Chiefland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in parts of Levy County fell to below freezing for around 3 hours.  The coldest temperature reported was 29 degrees at the COOP station at Chiefland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very steep lapse rates were in place atop rich boundary layer moisture with 500 mb temperatures from -11C to -14C.  This supported strong instability with afternoon SBCAPE values exceeding 3000 J/Kg.  Winds aloft were weak, so storms were mainly pulse in nature with brief-lived intense cores.  The strongest of these pulsating storms produced large hail and damaging winds.  Storms became semi-organized along a common outflow late in the day and shifted southward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near the intersection of Old Bainbridge and Valley Farm Road.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary stalled over central Florida setting up an environment that produced pulse thunderstorms south of the front.  One of these storms dropped half dollar sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports from trained spotters of nickle to half dollar sized hail in the Bradenton area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak trough moving over the region allowed for isolated thunderstorms to develop along the west coast of Florida and push east through the day.  One of these storms produced 1 inch hail stones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement estimated quarter sized hail near the intersection of County Roads 326 and 337.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying frontal boundary over the Florida peninsula combined with deep low level moisture to allow strong thunderstorms to develop right along the southwest Florida coast and slowly move inland.  One of these storms produced one inch hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a report of quarter sized hail at Peachland Boulevard and Harbor Boulevard in Port Charlotte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A waterspout moved onshore around Pensacola causing minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout moved onshore and caused minor damage to a fence...shed and house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered diurnal convection occurred for the last few days of the month.  An unusual event for July occurred in Wakulla county when a brief tornado touched down with one of the storms in the early evening hours.  The 00z KTAE sounding revealed a localized area of stronger low level shear in the area which likely contributed to this event occurring.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gulf county 911 call center reported power lines down in Overstreet.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another day of scattered afternoon pulse convection occurred across northwest Florida with a few storms once again becoming briefly severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and power line were blown down in Quincy.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The outer bands from Tropical Storm Debby located in the Northeast Gulf of Mexico continued to move over South Florida. Severe thunderstorms developed during the late morning into the afternoon with severe wind gusts and eight tornadoes were also spawned. The eight tornadoes that occurred over a span of four hours was the most number of tornadoes in one day over the southern Florida peninsula since October 14, 1964 when Hurricane Isbell also spawned eight tornadoes. All of the tornadoes were of EF0 intensity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Glades County Sheriff's Office reported a tornado crossing Bermont Road (SR 74) about a mile southwest of Palmdale. No known damage due to tornado moving over unpopulated area west of US 27 just west of the town of Palmdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Typically high heat and humidity levels lead to reports of heat exhaustion at a summer camp in Davie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot afternoon temperatures and high relative humidity led to health issues to six children at a summer camp in Davie. The children were taken to Cleveland Clinic and Memorial West hospitals after suffering from heat exhaustion. Heat index values across the area were around 100 degrees at the time of the incident. These values are normal for this time of year.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent southerly winds from the outer circulation of Tropical Storm Debby caused tidal flooding in the Everglades City area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent and strong southerly winds pushed water onshore over southern Collier County in the Everglades City area. Minor flooding occurred over several consecutive high tide cycles beginning on June 23rd, with the highest water levels occurring during the high tide cycle of the morning of June 26th. Streets in Everglades City were flooded, damaging roads and water pumps.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough of low pressure moved across South Florida and produced numerous strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public estimated wind gusts to 60 mph in Doral.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and persistent upper-level trough over the Gulf of Mexico continued to provide a moist and unstable air mass across south Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree branches of 3-4 inches in diameter were snapped near the intersection of NW 67 Ave and the Palmetto Expressway in Miami Lakes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and persistent upper-level trough over the Gulf of Mexico continued to provide a moist and unstable air mass across south Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel-size hail reported in Buckhead Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective vortex affected the forecast area during the afternoon hours of April 3rd.  This feature locally enhanced the wind profile and with a moderately unstable airmass in place, severe thunderstorms erupted over portions of northwest Florida and southwest Georgia.  One man was killed in Leon county when a tree fell on him.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a pickup on the road near the intersection of North Meridian and Orchard Pond Road.  The driver escaped uninjured, but the pickup was destroyed.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto power lines on Telogia Creek Road.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered afternoon and evening storms developed over South Florida in typically moist and unstable airmass along sea breeze and outflow boundaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of a funnel cloud west of Homestead were relayed by local media. The pictures and video generated a fair amount of media interest even though the funnel did not touch down or cause damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure moved across South Florida interacting with abundant tropical moisture to produce some record amounts of rainfall across the Miami-Dade County metropolitan region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was observed by motorists along the Sawgrass Expressway in NW metro Broward County. The funnel cloud was just west of the highway in an unpopulated area and extended very close to the ground, although there were no confirmed reports of a touchdown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon and evening thunderstorms developed in southwest flow associated with the outer circulation of Tropical Storm Beryl.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of funnel clouds observed just west of the metro areas of southern Palm Beach County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early start to the wet season caused by persistent low pressure over the Gulf of Mexico led to the improvement in drought conditions over interior South Florida and the Lake Okeechobee area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dry start to May was followed by an early start to the South Florida wet season which began during the second week of May. More frequent rains from this point forward led to drought conditions improving from severe to moderate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The outer bands from Tropical Storm Debby located in the Northeast Gulf of Mexico continued to move over South Florida. Severe thunderstorms developed during the late morning into the afternoon with severe wind gusts and eight tornadoes were also spawned. The eight tornadoes that occurred over a span of four hours was the most number of tornadoes in one day over the southern Florida peninsula since October 14, 1964 when Hurricane Isbell also spawned eight tornadoes. All of the tornadoes were of EF0 intensity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the Civil Air Patrol reported a funnel cloud at the Lantana Airport moving northward.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very steep lapse rates were in place atop rich boundary layer moisture with 500 mb temperatures from -11C to -14C.  This supported strong instability with afternoon SBCAPE values exceeding 3000 J/Kg.  Winds aloft were weak, so storms were mainly pulse in nature with brief-lived intense cores.  The strongest of these pulsating storms produced large hail and damaging winds.  Storms became semi-organized along a common outflow late in the day and shifted southward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down near Vernon.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level trough was present over the southeastern U.S. A weak surface low was located just offshore of the Florida panhandle.  A mesoscale convective vort max was also in the area.  SBCAPE values of 1500-2500 j/kg and delta thetae values of 25-35 contributed to several instances of severe storms with trees and power lines down across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown into power lines in Caryville.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front approached from AL and Ga through the day with numerous strong to severe storms developing across SE Ga and NE Fl by late morning then progressing eastward across the area through the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees and limbs were blown down west of Live Oak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front approached from AL and Ga through the day with numerous strong to severe storms developing across SE Ga and NE Fl by late morning then progressing eastward across the area through the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on photos, the NWS determined that an EF0 tornado caused wind damage (max winds around 75 mph) across the west side of Gainesville. A carport was damaged and  numerous trees were blown down along 196th Terrace Ave. Two homes were also damaged along the path. At least 3 dozen trees were snapped off along a portion of the path as well. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early start to the wet season caused by persistent low pressure over the Gulf of Mexico led to the improvement in drought conditions over interior South Florida and the Lake Okeechobee area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dry start to May was followed by an early start to the South Florida wet season which began during the second week of May. More frequent rains from this point forward led to drought conditions improving from severe to moderate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very steep lapse rates were in place atop rich boundary layer moisture with 500 mb temperatures from -11C to -14C.  This supported strong instability with afternoon SBCAPE values exceeding 3000 J/Kg.  Winds aloft were weak, so storms were mainly pulse in nature with brief-lived intense cores.  The strongest of these pulsating storms produced large hail and damaging winds.  Storms became semi-organized along a common outflow late in the day and shifted southward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell and completely blocked I-10 westbound between mile markers 177 and 178.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered diurnal convection occurred for the last few days of the month.  An unusual event for July occurred in Wakulla county when a brief tornado touched down with one of the storms in the early evening hours.  The 00z KTAE sounding revealed a localized area of stronger low level shear in the area which likely contributed to this event occurring.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Scotts Ferry Road at the intersection of Clements Road.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions continued across South Florida as the drier than normal winter pattern persisted across the area through March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High pressure led to limited rainfall across the area as few cold fronts passed through. Rainfall totals were mostly under an inch, except a few area which received slightly over an inch. This is about 30 to 50 percent of the normal rainfall for March. As a result, severe drought conditions continued over the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate east winds led to rip current formation along the beaches of Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two swimmers, a 65-year-old man and 15-year-old girl, were rescued from rip currents by lifeguards along Fort Lauderdale Beach near Tower 13 at the 900 block of North Atlantic Blvd. Both were taken to the hospital and recovered.||A high risk of rip currents was in place for all of Southeast Florida, although Fort Lauderdale Ocean Rescue personnel were only flying yellow flags.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure area over South Florida in combination with a low level trough moving across the area led to heavy showers moving onshore during the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Part of a roof collapsed in a home in Hallandale Beach, injuring an 81-year-old man. Heavy rains had fallen over the area since the previous night, with an estimated 2 inches of rain over a 12 hour period. Winds were gusting to 40 mph during the day as well. ||The roof was already damaged and had leaked in prior events.

One man was killed in Leon county when a tree fell on him.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tallahassee Regional Airport measured a wind gust of 256/54 knots at 2:59 pm EDT and a wind gust of 274/52 knots at 3:00 pm EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain developed over the area during the morning and continued into the afternoon. The heaviest rain fell across southern Escambia county. Storm total rainfall amounts were between 10 to 20 inches. This caused widespread flooding in many areas especially around Pensacola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The County Line wildfire, likely caused by arson, developed near the Baker and Columbia county line in the Osceola National Forest on April 5th. On April 7, the fire had burned 4752 acres. The fire caused heavy smoke along Interstate 10, and along State Road 100 between Lake City and Lake Butler on April 8th midday. On the 10th, the fire has burned about 25,000 acres. By the 11th, the fire had burned about 35,000 acres. Smoke from the fire caused visibility restrictions below 1 mile along Interstate 10 during the afternoon and evening between Macclenny and Jacksonville. By the 19th, the fire was about 80% contained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The County Line fire in the Osceola National Forest burned about 35,000 acres from April 4th through the 19th. Significant visibility restrictions occurred from the fire across NE Florida as well as poor breathing conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mean layer troughing positioned over SE Georgia as upper level divergence increased within the right rear quadrant of the upper level jet stream. Lingering outflow boundaries and active sea breezes in concert with a very moist and unstable airmass combined with the dynamical convective triggers to support widespread flooding and isolated severe storms during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Naval Air Station Jacksonville measured a 12 hour rainfall of 3.84 inches. The one hour total of 3.45 inches occurred between 3 and 4 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mean layer troughing positioned over SE Georgia as upper level divergence increased within the right rear quadrant of the upper level jet stream. Lingering outflow boundaries and active sea breezes in concert with a very moist and unstable airmass combined with the dynamical convective triggers to support widespread flooding and isolated severe storms during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public measured 4.86 inches of rainfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four men had to be rescued from a strong rip current behind the Four Seasons Hotel at 2800 South Ocean Blvd in Palm Beach Florida. One man was taken to the JFK Medical Center where he was treated and released.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively strong area of low pressure moved through the region during the evening of February 18th and into the overnight hours. Widespread convection with some severe storms and areas of heavy rain accompanied this low pressure system.  Convection was aided by very strong wind shear and strong winds just above the surface (around 40-45 knots at 1500-2000 ft AGL).  Although instability was minimal, it was still sufficient for some storms to produce severe wind gusts at the surface.  In addition, some training of storms occurred with very heavy rainfall amounts in just a few hours.  As a result, flash flooding occurred over portions of the eastern Florida panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were underwater across portions of Jackson county, including around Highway 90 near Cottonwood as well as Old U.S. Road 162 North.  Rainfall rates were reported around 4 inches per hour in neighboring Holmes county.  Widespread rainfall amounts of 3 to 5 inches occurred in the area in just a few hours.  The result was flash flooding of roads causing road closures as reported by the 911 call center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall line moved over the Suwannee River Vally during the evening. Instability was marginal, however low level winds and speed shear were very strong with the 850 mb jet 50-60 knots. A nose of higher instability moved inland from Apalachee Bay and coincided over the Suwannee River Valley, and these convective ingredients phased to produce scattered pockets of wind damage with discrete rotating cells during the evening. Trees were blown down across portions of Suwannee, Hamilton and Alachua counties during this evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees blown down west of Live Oak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lingering surface front over north Florida and a surge of tropical moisture from the east combined to produce widespread areal flooding over coastal Duval during the morning from Ft. Caroline to Jacksonville Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 7:30 am, the NWS employee reported flooding near Marsh Hammock Drive East. At 8:49 am, significant flooding was reported in areas near 10th Avenue South and 8th Avenue in Jacksonville Beach. The water depth was 1 to 1.5 ft deep. South Beach Parkway was flooded an impassable. At 8:55 am, there was significant flooding near 3rd Street and 14th Avenue in Jacksonville Beach. There area was barely passable. There was also significant flooding near 3rd St and 6th Avenue North. The right lane was closed. At 9:30 am, there was significant flooding in areas near 3rd St and 18th Avenue North. Water was entering structures, including the Five Guys restaurant and Good Year Tire Company.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly strong upper level trough and cold front swept across the region on the evening of January 26th.  SBCAPE values increased to around 1000 j/kg across portions of the Florida panhandle and southeast Alabama ahead of the approaching squall line.  Bulk shear values also increased to around 50 knots over the western zones.  The Storm Prediction Center issued tornado watches valid through the evening hours for most of the area.  However, instability decreased with eastward extent, and lapse rates were relatively poor.  The squall line gradually weakened as it moved eastward, and only a couple of reports of severe weather were received across the area with no tornadoes reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The broadcast media reported that a power pole fell onto a house on Vogue Drive just south of the intersection of Capital Circle and Apalachee Parkway.  Emergency management reported that an oak tree fell onto a car in the same area near the intersection of Capital Circle and Apalachee Parkway.  Monetary damage was estimated.

Upper level temperatures around -7 to -8 degrees Celcius and precipitable water values just under 2 inches provided enough instability and moisture for scattered severe storms to develop and persist along the sea breezes and outflows during the afternoon and evening as the upper trough passed over the area aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across State Road 11 about 2 miles north of the Volusia County Line. The cost of debris removal was estimated.

The sea breezes and outflow triggered scattered severe storms in the afternoon and evening. Slow and erratic storm motion promoted locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down east of Dowling Park and Luraville which caused partial road blockage. The time of damage was based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively strong area of low pressure moved through the region during the evening of February 18th and into the overnight hours. Widespread convection with some severe storms and areas of heavy rain accompanied this low pressure system.  Convection was aided by very strong wind shear and strong winds just above the surface (around 40-45 knots at 1500-2000 ft AGL).  Although instability was minimal, it was still sufficient for some storms to produce severe wind gusts at the surface.  In addition, some training of storms occurred with very heavy rainfall amounts in just a few hours.  As a result, flash flooding occurred over portions of the eastern Florida panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated wind gusts near 60 mph near De Funiak Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rough seas of 8 to 10 feet behind a cold front along with a building northerly swell led to very rough seas and hazardous marine conditions over the Atlantic waters of Southeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 45-foot commercial diving boat with 23 people aboard capsized and broke apart just outside Hillboro Inlet around 1230 PM EST on Thanksgiving Day, November 22nd. Multiple water rescues were performed. Four people were transported to local hospitals with one fatality reported.||It is not known what caused the boat to capsize but passengers reported rough seas that sent a large wave over the rear of the boat causing it to take on water and roll.||Witnesses also report a large wave causing the incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very moist southwest winds developed across east-central Florida well in advance of a cold front and associated upper level disturbance.  Strong daytime heating, combined with moderate low-level wind shear, resulted in numerous strong storms, two of which produced tornadoes. The first tornado began as a waterspout over Lake Apoka, then crossed the northeast shore of the lake and remained on the ground for a short distance. Although the waterspout/tornado affected a rural area, several citizens witnessed the event. The second tornado impacted a mobile home park and adjacent wooded area in Edgewater, then became a waterspout as it crossed the intracoastal river, before moving across the barrier island as a tornado. Significant damage occurred at the mobile home park, with only minor damage on the barrier island. Several funnel clouds were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports were received from the public of funnel clouds, west of Fort Pierce.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very moist southwest winds developed across east-central Florida well in advance of a cold front and associated upper level disturbance.  Strong daytime heating, combined with moderate low-level wind shear, resulted in numerous strong storms, two of which produced tornadoes. The first tornado began as a waterspout over Lake Apoka, then crossed the northeast shore of the lake and remained on the ground for a short distance. Although the waterspout/tornado affected a rural area, several citizens witnessed the event. The second tornado impacted a mobile home park and adjacent wooded area in Edgewater, then became a waterspout as it crossed the intracoastal river, before moving across the barrier island as a tornado. Significant damage occurred at the mobile home park, with only minor damage on the barrier island. Several funnel clouds were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began at US Highway 1 in Edgewater and continued northeast through the Terra Mar Village Mobile Home Park to Alice Street and Jones Fish Camp Road, then moved onto the intracoastal river as a waterspout (see 1726 LST waterspout episode). A total of 62 homes were damaged (17 with minor damage and 45 with major damage), impacting carports, awnings, roofs, power poles and lines, and numerous trees. Over 100 trees (many very large) between the mobile home park and the intracoastal river were snapped near the ground or uprooted. Metal from the mobile homes were carried far downstream. The frame of a mobile home was wrapped several times around the base of a tree. The tornado produced high-end EF0 damage within the mobile home park, then strengthened to a high-end EF1 tornado with a widening damage swath along Jones Fish Camp Road and upon approach to the intracoastal waterway. There were two very minor injuries, both treated at the scene. Maximum winds estimated at 90-110 mph. A motorist travelling on US Highway 1 videotaped the tornado crossing the highway in front of him, with debris evident within the circulation as it reached the mobile home park. Several other citizens and a public safety official also witnessed the tornado. DI3 DOD4 EXP; DI3 DOD8 LB-EXP; DI27 DOD4 EXP; DI28 DOD4 EXP.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado which impacted Edgewater from 1725-1726 LST, continued northeast across the intracoastal waterway (1726-1729 LST), then moved across the narrow barrier island at Bethune Beach as a small, weak tornado. Several power lines were downed by the tornado along Highway A1A and small debris from palm trees and shrubs were observed along the path. A citizen observed the tornado cross the barrier island, from the second story of his home just to the north. Maximum winds estimated at 55-65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mean layer troughing positioned over SE Georgia as upper level divergence increased within the right rear quadrant of the upper level jet stream. Lingering outflow boundaries and active sea breezes in concert with a very moist and unstable airmass combined with the dynamical convective triggers to support widespread flooding and isolated severe storms during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread areal flooding occurred across downtown Jacksonville from the Westside of town to Mandarin, and northward to Arlington and the Northside. At 3:45 pm, Duval County Emergency Management reported cars were stranded due to flooded roads throughout the San Marco area. At 3:55 pm, law enforcement reported the Powers Road from Toledo Road to University Blvd was closed due to flooding. At 4 pm, emergency management reported flooding along Blanding Blvd near Interstate 295 in Argyle. At 4 pm, emergency management also stated a roof collapsed at an apartment complex along San Jose Blvd due to heavy rainfall. At 4:06 pm, the media reported a car was trapped by flood waters in Argyle. At 4:18 pm, the media reported a home was flooded in Ortega with water rescues by fire officials. At 4:18 pm, emergency management reported water was entering a home in Englewood. The car of the homeowner was flooded and there was widespread street flooding in the area. At 4:26 pm, a spotter reported a drainage ditch overflowed and caused flooding on Harlow Blvd. The spotter also measured 3.2 inches of storm total rainfall. At 4:53 pm, emergency management reported a vehicle stuck in flood waters in San Marco. At 5:01 pm, emergency management reported a sinkhole behind an apartment complex near Naval Air Station Jacksonville. At 5:11 pm, emergency management reported water entered a home near Bowden Road close to University Blvd. At 5:12 pm, emergency management reported water entered a home in San Marco. AT 5:12 pm, emergency management reported street flooding over Sabena Road near University Blvd. At 5:15 pm, the media reported significant inundation due to flooding at the intersection of McCoys Creek Blvd and Stockton Street in downtown Jacksonville. At 5:15 pm, the media reported inundation due to flood waters near the intersection of Roosevelt Blvd and South Edgewood Ave in the Avondale area of downtown Jacksonville. At 5:16 pm, emergency management reported flooded streets in southside Jacksonville between Atlantic Blvd and Beach Blvd near University Blvd South. At 5:18 pm, emergency management reported a vehicle stuck in flood waters in the southbound lane of the approach to the Buckman Bridge. At 5:25 pm, emergency management reported that north Main Street and Zoo Parkway were closed due to flooding and the concrete cracking underneath an overpass and shifting. At 5:27 pm, emergency management reported a sinkhole on University Blvd North and Arlington Road. At 5:30 pm, emergency management reported a vehicle stuck in flood waters in the San Jose area of Jacksonville. The cost of damage was estimated, and it is likely grossly underestimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Air Force Wind Tower 511 at the Kennedy Space Center recorded a wind gust of 58 mph as a severe thunderstorm moved overhead.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was snapped across the road in an apartment complex near the intersection of Capital Circle NE and Miccosukee Road.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon convection and above normal precipitable water values led to heavy rainfall and a couple of strong to severe storms across portions of northwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A single tree was blown down on a power line near Freeport.  No other wind damage was reported in the area.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed along the east coast sea breeze with a couple of them becoming strong enough to produce nickel-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A citizen reported nickel sized hail near Exit 272 on the Florida Turnpike near Oakland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy showers and isolated thunderstorms developed ahead of an upper trough which moved through east central Florida during the day. One storm acquired rotation during the afternoon hours and produced a short-lived tornado in a rural area. A funnel cloud was also spotted in association with the storm shortly thereafter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off-duty sheriff's office deputy sighted a large funnel cloud for several minutes northeast of his location at the 8000 block of State Road 70 West.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The outer bands of Tropical Storm Beryl began to move over west central Florida on the 27th, before the center made landfall near Jacksonville early on the 28th.  Beryl then became a tropical depression less than 12 hours later as it curved northward to the Georgia border.  Beryl created onshore flow in west central Florida leading to isolated areas of heavy rain and tornadoes.||In Levy county, wind gusts of up to 38 knots with sustained winds of 20 to 25 knots were measured.  Rainfall of 1.25 inches was measured at the COOP station at Usher Tower.||In Citrus county, sustained winds of around 20 knots were measured across the county.  Rainfall of 3.98 inches was measured at the COOP station at Inverness 3 SE.||In Hernando county, sustained winds of 25 to 30 knots were measured across the county.  Rainfall of three to five inches was measured, with 5.00 inches measured at the COOP station at Brooksville - Chin Hill.  Radar rainfall estimates were as high as 8 inches in some parts of the county.||In Sumter County, rainfall of 2.83 inches was measured at the COOP site at Bushnell.||In Pasco County, sustained winds of 20 to 25 knots were measured across the county.  The COOP site at St. Leo measured 3.31 inches of rain.||In Pinellas County, thunderstorm wind gusts of up to 48 knots, with sustained wind gusts of 20 to 25 knots were measured.  Rainfall an inch or more were measured over the northern half of the county, with 1.12 inches measured at the COOP site at Tarpon Springs.||In Hillsborough County, thunderstorm wind gusts of up to 38 knots with sustained winds of around 25 knots were measured across the county.  Rainfall of around one inch or more were measured over most of the county, with 1.60 inches measured at the COOP station at Hillsborough River State Park.||In Polk County, thunderstorm wind gusts of up to 40 knots with sustained winds of around 30 knots were measured across the county.  The highest rainfall measured was 1.12 inches at the COOP site at Lakeland.||In Manatee County, sustained winds of around 20 knots were measured across the county.  Rainfall of over an inch were measured around the county, with 1.86 inches measured at the COOP station at Fort Green 12 WSW.||Over southwest Florida, sustained winds of around 20 knots were measured, with rainfall generally less than one inch were measured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A narrow heavy rain band flooded parts of County Road 581 between Brooksville to Inverness.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms occurred as low to mid-level southwest winds brought a warm and very moist air mass into east central Florida. Much cooler and drier air above this moisture laden air allowed for vigorous storm development which acted to produce severe wind gusts and hail in many of the thunderstorms that developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter observed quarter to half dollar sized hail near the intersection of Buckles Road and State Road 40, in Pierson.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Glades County Sheriffs Office reported a possible tornado in the Lakeport area with an estimated radar time of 250 AM EDT. Further investigation revealed that this was not a tornado but straight line thunderstorm wind gusts of 60-70 mph. A double-wide mobile home was tipped over but the unit was not tied down or secured. Another mobile home sustained roof damage but had already been weakened by previous unrepaired damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree of 12-18 inches in diameter was snapped at the intersection of Nob Hill Rd. and Cleary Blvd. in Plantation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a mid-level trough over the Gulf of Mexico. A few of these storms became strong to severe over portions of Brevard and Lake Counties, producing severe winds which damaged trees, and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A citizen reported a 60 mph thunderstorm wind gust on their home weather station near Pinetree Gardens in Titusville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck two trees at the Crane Lakes Golf Course in Port Orange. A 55 year old woman who was standing nearby was indirectly struck and taken to a nearby medical center where she was admitted in serious condition.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A SKYWARN spotter observed nickel sized hail as two strong thunderstorms merged together near Indian Harbour Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large mesoscale convective system moved southward across southern Georgia and southeast Alabama during the late evening and overnight hours of July 1st.  Strong instability was present with observed SBCAPE values around 5,000 j/kg on 00z soundings.  Activity gradually weakened as it approached northwest Florida, but a few areas did receive tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported trees and power lines down in northern Walton county.  At least 4 trees were blown down.  The report was obtained via facebook.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Beryl impacted east central Florida during May 27-29. Rainbands directly related to the tropical storm impacted mainly areas north of a line from Orlando to Cape Canaveral. Indirect impacts occurred farther south as additional rainbands developed during daytime heating and produced scattered thunderstorms with heavy rain, gusty winds, and an isolated short-lived tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms formed along the sea breeze boundary within the far outer circulation of Tropical Storm Beryl. One of the storms produced a brief tornado in Lyngate Park and near the Saint Lucie Medical Center near Port Saint Lucie. Estimated sustained winds were around 65 mph, consistent with low-end EF0 damage. Minor damage was sustained to the roofs of two homes, several fences were knocked over, and a few small trees were downed. The tornado was witnessed by a Saint Lucie County Sheriff Officer and damage assessment was relayed from Saint Lucie County Emergency Management. No injuries or fatalities occurred.

One of these storms became strong across southern Brevard County, producing penny to nickel sized hail across southern portions of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A citizen reported penny sized hail north of Post Road and just west of US Highway 1 in Melbourne as a strong thunderstorm moved overhead.

Sporadic wind damage was found along the storm track, consisting of isolated debris and vegetation blown-downs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm developed as westerly winds interacted with the east coast sea breeze west of Port St. Lucie. This created enhanced near-surface wind shear in an unstable environment, which caused a brief weak EF0 tornado (landspout) to touchdown. No damage or injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm intensified along the east coast sea breeze and produced a weak brief EF0 tornado (landspout) in a produce field off of Glades Cut Off Road west of Port St. Lucie. The tornado quickly crossed the road before lifting. Several photos were obtained from a local media outlet. An Emergency Management official confirmed that no damage or injuries occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Breezy onshore winds occurred along the Volusia County coastline as a result of a tight pressure gradient between a ridge of high pressure over the southeastern United States and a trough of low pressure located over the local western Atlantic. These winds led to rough surf and long-period northeast swells along the Volusia County coastline. Two swimmers, an hour apart and at different beaches, were imperiled by the dangerous ocean conditions. An elderly man suffered a heart attack and died while being rescued from the surf by life guards. A young teenager died from an asthma attack while fighting the rough surf. Autopsies revealed the two males did not die from drowning, therefore the deaths were listed as indirectly related to the rough surf and strong rip currents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 66 year old man became unconscious and subsequently died of a heart attack while being rescued from rough surf and strong rip currents at Ormond Beach.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 14 year old male was pulled out to sea by a rip current at New Smyrna Beach. He suffered an asthma attack and died while in the water. The rough surf and rip currents pulled him under water. His body was not recovered until the following morning.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A SKYWARN spotter observed a funnel cloud crossing the Indian River and moving east over US Highway 192 in Indialantic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed on seabreeze collisions, one of which produced a brief tornado touchdown over Pinellas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lealman Fire Department reported damage to an older hotel on the 4800 block of 34th Street North.  The corner of the roof was taken off and damaged five cars in the parking lot.  Damage in the area was isolated to this building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of storms moved northeast across northern St. Lucie County. One of the storms produced a very brief EF0 tornado on Indrio Road in Ft. Pierce, damaging the roofs of two condos and a fence before quickly lifting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The very brief EF0 tornado impacted one condominium building within the Indian Pines Village. The tornado stripped several sections of plywood from one roof of a condo and another unit sustained a small hole in the roof and the front entrance awning was peeled back. An eyewitness within the condo village observed a funnel cloud to the west touch down and damage two condo units within the same building. After striking the condo units, roof debris was seen being lifted aloft and swirling. Roofing debris was carried eastward as the tornado quickly lifted. Debris fell to the ground and onto other condo roofs farther to the east-northeast. Debris also fell into a small pond just downstream, and into another pond and an adjacent wooded area at least 500 feet to the northeast. St. Lucie County Emergency Management assisted by surveying the damage and provided details of the event and damage photos. DI5 DOD2 LB.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that moved across South Florida during the morning and early afternoon. Approximately 4,000 customers lost power in Collier County, with an estimated 5,800 in Broward and Miami-Dade counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of trees and power lines downed in the Gulf Shore Boulevard and Moorings Country Club area of Naples. This damage was not directly related to the waterspout that made landfall near 1901 Gulf Shore Blvd., but instead likely from downburst winds associated with the same storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While Tropical Storm Debby was located over the northeast Gulf of Mexico, numerous strong rain bands rotated across east-central Florida. One particular persistent, mini-supercell produced three tornadoes as it traveled north from Okeechobee to Brevard County. The tornadoes occurred over rural areas of Osceola and Brevard Counties, which limited damage. Another mini-supercell moved north from Polk County and produced discontinuous tornado damage along a 3.7 mile path in southeast Lake County. A linear squall line behind the mini-supercells caused a localized area of property damage in northwest Osceola County. A canoe capsized on an inland lake in Lake County due to gusty winds and choppy conditions on the water, resulting in a drowning. Multi-day rainfall totals exceeded four inches across portions of Seminole, Orange, Volusia and Lake Counties, with maximum accumulations of 7-9 inches near Pierson, Lady Lake, and Astor Park in Lake County. No significant flooding occurred due to abnormally dry soil conditions prior to the rain event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first of three tornadoes associated with a Tropical Storm Debby mini-supercell that traveled from northern Okeechobee County to northern Brevard County occurred at Yeehaw Junction. The tornado touched down in a wooded area south-southwest of Highway 441, about 1 mile south of the State Road 60 intersection then traveled north for 3.1 miles, crossing Highway 441, State Road 60, and the Florida Turnpike. Considerable tree damage, mainly to pines and small brush occurred along the path. The tornado moved between the turnpike toll booth and an adjacent vacant motel, producing minor structural damage and downing trees. Debris impacted and damaged a parked car within the toll booth parking lot. One vehicle, driving on the turnpike, sustained damage from a falling tree. The damage location was also consistent with a very strong rotational signature, reflectivity hook echo and tornado debris signature on dual-pol radar data. DI28 DOD2 EXP, DI6 DOD2 LB.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface trough of low pressure was located near Palm Beach County and extending east into the Bahamas leading to scattered thunderstorms in the afternoon across South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small trees uprooted along with broken tree branches and outdoor furniture tossed about in northern sections of Boca Raton near Spanish River Drive. Boat on Lake Rogers registered a 57 mph wind gust, with higher winds likely to the west.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a weak surface boundary moving down the Florida peninsula and the Lake Okeechobee lake breeze helped to spark storms north and east of Lake Okeechobee during the afternoon.  Cold late spring/early summer mid level temperatures allowed for the formation of severe hail in the storms over Martin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SKYWARN Spotter reported estimated dime to quarter-sized hail around Taylor Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rainfall from Tropical Storm Debby at the end of the month helped to end the drought across northwest Florida.

Drought conditions ranged from D2 severe status to D4 exceptional status across the area to start the month.  The D4 conditions were confined to the southeast big bend, including portions of Taylor, Lafayette, and Dixie counties.  Fortunately, the remnants of Tropical Storm Beryl impacted the southeast big bend at the end of the month with heavy amounts of rain, and the drought status there improved from D4 to D3.

Afternoon thunderstorms spread north into Martin and Okeechobee counties just to the north of a developing trough and one storm produced a funnel cloud.  Upper level temperatures were still cold enough for storms to produce severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SKYWARN Spotter observed penny-sized hail for 10 minutes near the intersection of Kanner Hwy and US-1 in Stuart.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms spread north into Martin and Okeechobee counties just to the north of a developing trough and one storm produced a funnel cloud.  Upper level temperatures were still cold enough for storms to produce severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported quarter-sized hail which lasted for about 15 minutes southwest of Palm City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms spread north into Martin and Okeechobee counties just to the north of a developing trough and one storm produced a funnel cloud.  Upper level temperatures were still cold enough for storms to produce severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SKYWARN Spotter reported quarter-sized hail lasting 10 minutes in Palm City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms spread north into Martin and Okeechobee counties just to the north of a developing trough and one storm produced a funnel cloud.  Upper level temperatures were still cold enough for storms to produce severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Saint Lucie County Public Safety Officer observed a funnel cloud about 7 miles south of their location in Port Saint Lucie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low was over Alabama with an active southwest flow regime over SE Ga and NE Fl. A backdoor surface front was sliding SSW from Savannah. The combination of these convective ingredients combined with daytime heating over an unstable air mass produced scattered severe storms over the area during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down and a trampoline blown on a fence in the Timbercreek area of Yulee. The time of the event was based on radar. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low was over Alabama with an active southwest flow regime over SE Ga and NE Fl. A backdoor surface front was sliding SSW from Savannah. The combination of these convective ingredients combined with daytime heating over an unstable air mass produced scattered severe storms over the area during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large trees were blown down near Callahan according to the Nassau Record newspaper. A large pine tree fell on a home on Dornbush Road and another tree fell across a cedar fence at a home on Lem Turner Road near Lisa Drive. The time of the event was based on radar. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved into central Florida as it dissipated, fueling isolated thunderstorms that produced one inch hail.  A lightning strike from one of the storms struck and injured a 27 year old man.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported near Lake Lowery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Beryl impacted east central Florida during May 27-29. Rainbands directly related to the tropical storm impacted mainly areas north of a line from Orlando to Cape Canaveral. Indirect impacts occurred farther south as additional rainbands developed during daytime heating and produced scattered thunderstorms with heavy rain, gusty winds, and an isolated short-lived tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A nineteen-year-old male ventured onto a submerged sandbar and was carried into deeper water by wave action and currents. The event was witnessed by an additional swimmer and the victim's body was found the next day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Beryl affected the southeast big bend of Florida with very heavy rainfall, in excess of 12 inches in some areas.  However, due to the ongoing extreme drought across the area, no flooding of significance was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total of 8.09 inches through 5 am EDT was measured on the Suwannee River Water Management District Rain Gauge located near Cooks Hammock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist air mass fueled seabreeze thunderstorms over southwest Florida.  Some of the thunderstorms produced damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management damage survey found approximately 13 homes with minor roof and fence damage and one home with about a third of the roof torn off. Additionally, a large oak tree with no root ball was downed. Damage occurred in an east to west path from around Oxwood Street to North Port Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The east coast sea breeze as well as outflow boundaries from other storms collided with a southwest to northeast oriented boundary over northern portions of East Central Florida. The result was a number of clusters of storms which produced sub-severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A citizen used Twitter to report pea to penny-sized hail covering the ground at his location in Howey-in-the-Hills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While Tropical Storm Debby was located over the northeast Gulf of Mexico, numerous strong rain bands rotated across east-central Florida. One particular persistent, mini-supercell produced three tornadoes as it traveled north from Okeechobee to Brevard County. The tornadoes occurred over rural areas of Osceola and Brevard Counties, which limited damage. Another mini-supercell moved north from Polk County and produced discontinuous tornado damage along a 3.7 mile path in southeast Lake County. A linear squall line behind the mini-supercells caused a localized area of property damage in northwest Osceola County. A canoe capsized on an inland lake in Lake County due to gusty winds and choppy conditions on the water, resulting in a drowning. Multi-day rainfall totals exceeded four inches across portions of Seminole, Orange, Volusia and Lake Counties, with maximum accumulations of 7-9 inches near Pierson, Lady Lake, and Astor Park in Lake County. No significant flooding occurred due to abnormally dry soil conditions prior to the rain event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The second of three tornadoes associated with a Tropical Storm Debby mini-supercell that traveled from northern Okeechobee County to northern Brevard County produced a short damage path through a wooded area within the Kempfer Ranch and Deseret Ranch in far eastern Osceola County, about 1 mile north of Highway 192. The damage path cut through an area of mainly pine trees, damaged a fence, and displaced a pile of aluminum pipes downstream. The damage location was also consistent with a very strong rotational signature, reflectivity hook echo and tornado debris signature on dual-pol radar data. An Osceola County Emergency Management Official surveyed the damage path and provided details and damage photos. DI28 DOD2 LB-EXP.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon convection and above normal precipitable water values led to heavy rainfall and a couple of strong to severe storms across portions of northwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Perry-Foley airport K40J measured a daily rainfall total of 3.63 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon convection and above normal precipitable water values led to heavy rainfall and a couple of strong to severe storms across portions of northwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Suwannee River Water Management District rain gauge at Cabbage Grove measured a daily rainfall total of 3.46 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon convection and above normal precipitable water values led to heavy rainfall and a couple of strong to severe storms across portions of northwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CWOP site CW8654 located at Horseshoe Beach measured a rainfall total of 6.04 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms associated with a tropical wave over South Florida moved north into east-central Florida and organized into a tropical squall line, with severe winds along the leading edge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter's Davis Pro weather station measured a 68 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms associated with a tropical wave over South Florida moved north into east-central Florida and organized into a tropical squall line, with severe winds along the leading edge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Florida Water Management District site S65CW measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph along the Kissimmee River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms associated with a tropical wave over South Florida moved north into east-central Florida and organized into a tropical squall line, with severe winds along the leading edge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station NLWF1 measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms formed along the east coast sea breeze and subsequently produced several prominent outflow boundaries.  Along one of the outflow boundaries, a particularly deep storm developed over Seminole County and produced up to nickel-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported nickel-sized hail on Sandalwood Way in Longwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subtle upper level shortwave affected the area from north to south during the day.  The airmass was unstable with SBCAPE values in the 2500-3000 j/kg range during the afternoon hours with high delta thetae values.  This provided a favorable environment for strong to severe thunderstorms with damaging wind being the main threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gulf county 911 call center reported that a couple of trees were blown down north of White City.  Monetary damage was estimated.

The airmass was unstable with SBCAPE values in the 2500-3000 j/kg range during the afternoon hours with high delta thetae values.  This provided a favorable environment for strong to severe thunderstorms with damaging wind being the main threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down within the city limits of De Funiak Springs.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subtle upper level shortwave affected the area from north to south during the day.
