The tide gauge at Fort Myers measured a peak tide of 3.98 feet MLLW on afternoon of the 25th. Subtracting the predicted astronomical tide, the highest storm surge was calculated as 3.09 feet on the afternoon of the 25th. Lighthouse Road in Sanibel suffered erosion and was closed by Sanibel Police on the morning of the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four people, some vacationing from Alabama became caught in rip currents as they were swimming in the Gulf of Mexico after Tropical Storm Debby.  A relative who attempted to save them became the fifth person caught in the rip currents.  Bystanders pulled all five people back to shore, but one of them, a 41 year old woman, was unconscious and not breathing, and died as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaac crossed eastern Cuba on August 25th and moved northwest through the Florida Keys and into the eastern Gulf of Mexico.  A Tropical Storm Watch was issued for Pinellas County south through Lee County on the morning of the 25th and was extended north through Levy County that afternoon.  Tropical Storm Warnings were issued overnight on the 25th/early morning on the 26th for the entire area.  Additionally, the Governor of Florida issued a declaration of a state of emergency on the 25th.  Isaac stayed off the western Florida coast and eventually made landfall in southeastern Louisiana at Hurricane strength.||The impacts of Isaac over west central and southwest Florida were minimal.  One waterspout was spotted over the Tampa Bay and moved inland as a brief tornado with minor damage.  ||The heaviest rain fell in Highlands County, with over 6 inches of rain across most of the county, and minor road flooding reported.  The highest storm total rain report for the area was 6.71 inches at the CoCoRaHS station 3.9 SSW Sebring.  Additional flooding occurred along the Myakka River near Venice in Sarasota County, where a mobile home park sustained minor flooding. ||Tropical storm force wind gusts were confined to coastal area, with wind gusts of 34 to 46 knots reported along the coast.||Storm surge was fairly minor, with less than 3 feet of surge at all forecast points.  At Fort Myers, 2.31 feet of surge was reported, or 3.22 feet of storm tide (MLLW).  At Saint Petersburg,  2.48 feet of surge was reported, or 3.90 feet of storm tide (MLLW).  At McKay Bay Entrance, 2.56 feet of surge was reported, or 4.19 feet of storm tide (MLLW).  At Cedar Key, 2.91 feet of storm surge was reported, or 5.77 feet of storm tide (MLLW).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Myakka River at Ramblers Mobile Home Park flooded it's banks and caused minor flooding to several mobile homes. Water rose up to the doorsteps of several units and flooded parking lots and grassy areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled over northern Florida with a prefrontal trough ahead of the front sliding down the peninsula, along with a line of thunderstorms.  One of these storms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts knocked down an elongated temporary tent being setup as a tunnel for the Republican National Convention. Wind gusts in the area were reported as high as 45 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying stationary boundary over Central Florida provided a focus for deep low level moisture resulting in showers and isolated thunderstorms.  One of the storms produced a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A SKYWARN spotter observed a funnel cloud along I-75 near Nokomis.  Pictures of the funnel were widely circulated by broadcast media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture from southwest flow fueled numerous thunderstorms ahead of an approaching cold front. One of these thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts and heavy rain pushed over one shallow rooted tree and caused minor damage to fencing and pool cage screens.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A humid and unstable environment fueled seabreeze thunderstorms.  One of these storms produced small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a few reports of nickel sized hail from the public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying trough of low pressure over the eastern Gulf of Mexico produced thunderstorms with small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported nickel sized hail near Sebring.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure area moved across South Florida causing instability and combined with abundant tropical moisture to produce strong to severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a wind gust of 59 mph measured at a mesonite site at the Broward County School Tech and Support.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure area moved across South Florida causing instability and combined with abundant tropical moisture to produce strong to severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated a thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph 8 miles west of Delray Beach along U.S. Highway 441.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Sandy moved slowly northwest, parallel to the Florida coast, 200-250 miles offshore. Due to the very expansive wind fields associated with the hurricane, sustained tropical storm winds reached the east-central Florida beaches and adjacent portions of the barrier islands. Gusts to tropical storm force occurred throughout the coastal counties, with some interior counties also experiencing tropical storm gusts, especially during passing squalls. Large and pounding surf affected the beaches for six or more high tide cycles, during a period of high astronomical tides. By the end of the event, many of the beaches had sustained moderate to major beach erosion. The erosion damaged several home foundations/patios/pools, and endangered many other beach-front residences and two businesses. Numerous beach cross-overs and lifeguard towers were damaged and sand was washed onto coastal roadways and beach parking areas in several locations. Damage estimates for the east-central Florida beaches totalled 46 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A New Smyrna Beach mesonet site (XNSB) recorded sustained north winds of 43 mph and other spotter reports confirmed tropical storm winds along the immediate coast of Volusia County. Gusts of 50-55 mph occurred, especially during passing squalls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Seabreeze thunderstorms moved through west central Florida during the early afternoon.  One thunderstorm started a fire at an apartment building, that destroyed the structure and killed one resident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire burned down an apartment building at Garden Isles Apartments off North 56th Street and Sligh Avenue.  Investigators determined that the fire was most likely caused by a lightning strike.  One resident, a 58 year old woman, died in the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaac moved west-northwest across the Florida Straits south of the Florida Keys on August 26. The northern edge of the wind and rain area associated with Isaac affected the South Florida peninsula throughout the day on the 26th. Isaac continued on a west-northwest track into the Gulf of Mexico on the 27th with winds, rain and flooding continuing over parts of South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to severe flooding occurred over parts of northern metro Broward County. Hardest hit areas were in Lauderhill from Commercial Boulevard to NW 44 ST between University Drive and Pine Island Road where canals overflowed their banks and streets were flooded for a few days. A few homes sustained minor water damage. A few on-ramps to the Sawgrass Expressway were closed due to standing water. ||Rainfall amounts in these areas were in the 8-12 inch range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaac moved from near western Haiti and the eastern tip of Cuba late on August 24, 2012, through the Florida Straits, and into the southeast Gulf of Mexico through August 27.   The center passed within 40 nautical miles to the south southwest of Key West during the late afternoon hours of Sunday, August 26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The collective effects of Tropical Storm Isaac during the period of August 24 through 27, resulted in over 2.1 million dollars in damages for the Florida Keys of Monroe County.  Within the overall total, including damages and expenditures for Tropical Storm Isaac operations, over 200,000 dollars was directly associated with coastal flooding damage within the City of Key West and approximately 150,000 dollars due to wind damage. ||The center of Tropical Storm Isaac passed approximately 40 nautical miles to the south southwest of Key West the afternoon of August 26.  ||Maximum Storm Tides in the Middle Florida Keys of Monroe County were measured at 1.87 feet above mean lower low water at Vaca Key near the U.S. Coast Guard Station Marathon, with estimates up to 2.25 feet above mean lower low water on the oceanside at Marathon and Key Colony Beach.||The minimum pressure measured in the Middle Florida Keys of Monroe County was 1000.0 mb at an automated Citizen Weather Observing Station on Long Key.||Maximum sustained winds in the Middle Florida Keys of Monroe County were measured at 44 mph at an automated Citizen Weather Observing Station on Long Key, with peak gusts to 59 mph.||Rainfall totals in the Middle Keys of Monroe County ranged from 2.82 inches at the Marathon Florida Keys Airport to 3.83 inches at Curry Hammock State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough, partially associated with the remnants of Hurricane Isaac, affected the region.  Heavy rainfall and flooding occurred across Walton county.  Rainfall of 4-5 inches was spread out over a 24 hour period and water level rises were gradual, so flash flooding did not occur even though there were several areas of areal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Silas Road was closed due to areal flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The center of Tropical Storm Isaac moved over the Florida Straits south of the Florida Keys on Sunday, August 26th, passing just south of Key West. Rain bands and winds on the north side of the circulation of Isaac affected Southeast Florida throughout the day of the 26th and part of the 27th. Highest winds over land were recorded along and near the southeast Florida coast where the highest sustained winds ranged from 40-45 mph, with 25-35 mph sustained winds over most inland areas as well as over southwest Florida. Highest wind gusts ranged from 50-60 mph over most land areas to as high as 65 mph along the Atlantic coast and just offshore.||Three-day rainfall totals ending at 8 AM August 28th ranged from 5-7 inches across southeast Florida to 3-5 inches over interior and southwest Florida. The primary exception was over northern metro Broward County and much of Palm Beach County where 8 to 12 inches fell, with maximum amounts up to 15-18 inches from west of Boynton Beach to Wellington, The Acreage, Royal Palm Beach and Loxahatchee. These areas of highest rainfall amounts experienced severe flooding with communities cut off for several days after the storm. ||Maximum storm tide values were observed at 4.9 feet at Naples, with estimates of 5 to 7 feet along the southern Collier County coast from Goodland to Everglades City. Highest estimated inundation values of up to 3 feet above ground level were noted in Goodland and Everglades City. Major beach erosion was also observed along the Collier County beaches, with moderate beach erosion along the Atlantic beaches. ||All of the associated effects of Isaac in south Florida resulted in about $17.2 million in property damage. Specifically, Isaac's inland flood waters resulted in about $10 million in damages, mostly in Palm Beach and Broward counties. Flooding caused by storm tides along the coast in Collier County resulted in about $400 thousand in damage. Damage from beach erosion in Collier and Broward counties was estimated at $6 million. Wind damage was estimated at $750,000.||Approximately 113,000 customers lost power during the storm in South Florida.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of T.S. Sandy and strong high pressure over the central U.S. combined to create a very strong, northeast flow over NE Florida and the adjacent coastal waters from Friday Nov. 26th through late on the 27th. Tropical storm force winds were measured over the adjacent coastal waters which were under a Tropical Storm Warning. Tropical storm force winds were measured along the Flagler County coast on Friday evening, where a tropical storm warning extended up to St. Augustine. A tropical storm watch was in effect from St. Augustine to Fernandina Beach. Elevated surf and dangerous rip currents impact the northeast Florida coast. Extra safety precautions were taken by emergency response officials due to a larger population in Jacksonville for the annual Fl-Ga College Football Game.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: St. Johns County Emergency Management reported coastal flooding around 7:05 pm on the evening of the 26th in St. Augustine. Water was going over the seawall south of the Bridge of Lions. Protective actions were taken. At 7:28 pm that evening, the St. Augustine AWOS station at the airport reported a gust of 49 mph. At 10 pm that evening, the St. Augustine C-Man station reported winds sustained at 36 mph with gusts to 48 mph. At 11 pm that evening, the St. Augustine C-Man reported measured sustained winds of 34 kts (39 mph) with a gust to 44 kts (51 mph).

These winds impacted much of the Brevard County barrier islands as well as some eastern portions of the mainland. Gusts of 50-55 mph occurred, especially during passing squalls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough and cold front moved through the area. There was sufficient instability and shear for a few strong to severe thunderstorms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Perry police reported a brief tornado touchdown near the police station.  No damage occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Sandy moved north across the northwest Bahamas producing tropical storm force winds along the southeast coast of Florida including the adjacent Atlantic waters with significant beach erosion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum storm tide of 4.15 feet above mean lower low water (MLLW) was observed at Virginia Key on October 28th at 848 AM EDT, along with a maximum storm surge of 1.44 feet. Similar tide and surge levels were measured at the highest daily high tide during this period, generally between 7 and 9 AM. Minor flooding was noted in Miami Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Sandy moved north across the northwest Bahamas producing tropical storm force winds along the southeast coast of Florida including the adjacent Atlantic waters with significant beach erosion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Sandy began to affect the Palm Beach County coast and its adjacent Atlantic waters with tropical storm force winds during the evening of October 25th as it moved slowly north across the northwest Bahamas. Tropical storm force wind gusts were first observed along the coastal Palm Beach County region by early in the evening of October 25th. Several WeatherFlow sensors along and near the Palm Beach County coast recorded Tropical Storm force wind gusts during the evening of October 25th with a peak wind gust of 67 mph observed at Jupiter. However, as Hurricane Sandy continued to move slowly north and then northeast over the Atlantic waters north of the Bahamas through the 28th the main impact along the Palm Beach County coast were large northeast swells generated by the storm which pummeled the Southeast Florida coast with significant beach erosion and coastal flooding. Large breaking waves of possibly over 20 feet were estimated along the coast. As a result, major coastal flooding occurred with the most significant impacts experienced from central Palm Beach north, including the Manalapan area where beachfront structures were threatened by water intrusion. In all, there was an estimated $14 million in damage sustained in Palm Beach County. A total of 44,270 customers lost power.||A maximum storm tide of 5.2 feet above mean lower low water (MLLW) was observed at Lake Worth Pier on October 28th at 712 AM EDT along with a maximum storm surge of 2.28 feet on October 28th at 236 AM EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Sandy moved north across the northwest Bahamas producing tropical storm force winds along the southeast coast of Florida including the adjacent Atlantic waters with significant beach erosion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum storm tide of 4.15 feet above mean lower low water (MLLW) was observed at Virginia Key on October 28th at 848 AM EDT, along with a maximum storm surge of 1.44 feet. Similar tide and surge levels were measured at the highest daily high tide during this period, generally between 7 and 9 AM. Large breakers in excess of 10 feet combined with the high tide levels led to major beach erosion and coastal flooding, particularly in the area along A1A in Fort Lauderdale Beach between Las Olas Boulevard and NE 20 St. Water reached up to 2 blocks inland and sand up to 2 feet deep accumulated along A1A. That portion of the roadway was closed for several days, leading to significant damage. No damage estimate is available at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm air advection brought unseasonably high temperatures near 80 degress across inland northeast Florida as a short wave trough was approaching from the Gulf of Mexico. Warm surface temperatures combined with cool temperatures aloft form the passing short wave trough and increased lift from the passing vorticity lobe enhanced thunderstorm development over inland northeast Florida during the afternoon and early evening over northeast Florida. As the storms approached the Florida Atlantic coast, low level helicity was increased due to the east coast sea breeze which was lingering near the coast due to prevailing low level southwest flow. As one storm over Flagler county approached the coast, a mesocylone intensified and a tornado warning was issued. A touchdown was never confirmed and post analysis indicates that perhaps a lingering low level inversion near the area from a marine stratus layer and dense fog earlier in the day prevented descent of the rear flank downdraft. Locally heavy rainfall of 1.38 inches was reported in 40 minutes about 3 miles SSW of Micanopy, while heavy rainfall was also reported in Newberry and Gainesville where storm total rainfall amounts of about 2.5 inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down about 3 miles north of Hawthorne along County Road 219A.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary over northern Florida interacted with a weak tropical wave moving across the Florida peninsula to produce numerous strong thunderstorms.  Some of these storms produced small hail and gusty winds.   The trees that were downed in Citrus and Sumter Counties were reported as old with rot.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail was reported in Sumterville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure moving across the eastern Gulf of Mexico fueled thunderstorms over central Florida.  One of these storms produced a brief hail shower.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported nickel sized hail in the Temple Terrace area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough of low pressure moved through the area, bringing with it a pre-frontal squall line.  Although wind fields were strong, limited instability precluded a more widespread event.  Scattered pockets of damaging winds occurred, and these were limited to tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A single tree was blown down in the Blountstown with no other damage reported in Calhoun county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As Hurricane Sandy moved through the central and northwest Bahamas, frequent tropical storm force wind gusts and higher-than-normal tides occurred throughout the Florida Keys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm tide flooding occurred as strong northwest winds around the periphery of Hurricane Sandy piled water along the north and west shores of the Lower Florida Keys. Flooding of secondary streets and private roads of 4 to 6 inches' depth was noted along the northern end of Big Pine Key, Sugarloaf Key and Key West.  Minor damage occurred when a spa pump at ground level was flooded on Big Pine Key.  Tides gradually receded by the early morning hours of October 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable tropical airmass remained across South Florida producing scattered afternoon thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Palm Beach County Emergency Management relayed a report of a funnel cloud near Pierson Road and South Shore Boulevard in Wellington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As Hurricane Sandy moved through the central and northwest Bahamas, frequent tropical storm force wind gusts and higher-than-normal tides occurred throughout the Florida Keys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Sandy produced tropical storm force wind gusts in the Upper Florida Keys as it moved north through the central and northwest Bahamas.  Maximum sustained winds ranged from 25 to 32 mph from Key Largo through Upper Matecumbe Key, with peak gusts measured at 48 mph at Upper Matecumbe and 44 mph at Key Largo.  Very minor wind damage occurred, from isolated power outages due to tree limbs contacting power lines. Approximately 700 customers were without power on October 25th.  As Hurricane Sandy continued to move north of the Bahamas, strong northwest winds across the eastern Gulf of Mexico pushed higher-than-normal tides into Florida Bay, reaching up to 2.5 feet above mean sea level and causing flooding of secondary streets.  The tides remained high due to normally higher full moon spring tide through the early part of the following week.  No flood damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front very quickly pushed across the Florida peninsula early on the morning of the 21st, with a cool air mass filling in behind the front.  Although the coldest temperatures would not be recorded until the morning of the 23rd, temperatures over the Nature Coast briefly dropped below freezing early on the morning of the 22nd.||Inland Levy County recorded widespread areas with 3 hours of freezing temperatures.  The coldest temperature reported was 27 degrees at the mesonet site Inglis 2W.||Citrus County had several reports of temperatures dropping below freezing for up to 3 hours, mostly at inland locations.  The coldest temperature reported was 30 degrees at the COOP station at Inverness 3 SE.||Pasco County reported an isolated patch with temperatures falling to freezing for an hour or two.  The coldest temperature reported was 32 degrees at the AWOS station at Zephyrhills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inland Levy County recorded widespread areas with 3 hours of freezing temperatures.  The coldest temperature reported was 27 degrees at the mesonet site Inglis 2W.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Sandy moved north across the northwest Bahamas producing tropical storm force winds along the southeast coast of Florida including the adjacent Atlantic waters with significant beach erosion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Sandy began to affect the Miami-Dade County coast and its adjacent Atlantic waters with tropical storm force winds during the early morning of October 25th as it moved north across the northwest Bahamas. Virginia Key reported a sustained wind of 40 mph shortly after 0500 LST with gusts to 50 mph. Sustained tropical storm force winds were observed at several coastal observations through the course of the day with a peak gust of 58 knots...or 67 mph being observed at the Fowey Rocks C-Man Station at 1200 LST. A peak gust of 52 knots...or 60 mph was observed at the Turkey Point Nuclear Power Plant. As Hurricane Sandy continued to move north and then northeast over the Atlantic waters north of the Bahamas, large northeast swells generated by the storm pummeled the Southeast Florida coast through October 30th with beach erosion and coastal flooding reported along the northeast shore of Miami-Dade County including the communities of Sunny Isles, Bal Harbour and Surfside. Large breaking of waves estimated at around 10 feet were reported along the coast of Miami-Dade County.||A maximum storm tide of 4.15 feet above mean lower low water (MLLW) was observed at Virginia Key on October 28th at 848 AM, along with a maximum storm surge of 1.44 feet. ||Only minor wind damage was noted, primarily to vegetation. A total 57,870 customers lost power. Exact damage figures are unknown for Miami-Dade County but are believed to be primarily from beach erosion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough and cold front moved through the area. There was sufficient instability and shear for a few strong to severe thunderstorms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across a road in northern Walton county.  Monetary damage was estimated. Across the border in Geneva county, several trees were blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff's department reported quarter sized hail along Interstate 75.

Additionally, one or two inches of water flooded some ground floor units at the Richmond Hill Apartment Complex at the same intersection.  ||Road closures also occurred at 113th Avenue and 15th Street intersection due to a foot of standing water.||Near Florida Avenue and Busch Boulevard at the Floriland Business Center, around 30 cars were stuck in standing water from the heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough and surface cold front affected the area on September 17-18.  A few storms became severe across northwest Florida with some areas of wind damage. Lightning also sparked a few fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused a mobile home fire along Balkin Road with damage estimated at around $20,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough and surface cold front affected the area on September 17-18.  A few storms became severe across northwest Florida with some areas of wind damage. Lightning also sparked a few fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused a fire to a single story wood frame home on Celtic Road.  The Tallahassee Fire Department estimated damage at $55,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough, partially associated with the remnants of Hurricane Isaac, affected the region.  Heavy rainfall and flooding occurred across Walton county.  Rainfall of 4-5 inches was spread out over a 24 hour period and water level rises were gradual, so flash flooding did not occur even though there were several areas of areal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Open Gulf Street was closed due to areal flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Sandy moved north across the northwest Bahamas producing tropical storm force winds along the southeast coast of Florida including the adjacent Atlantic waters with significant beach erosion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Sandy began to affect the Broward County coast and its adjacent Atlantic waters with tropical storm force winds during the afternoon of October 25th as it moved slowly north across the northwest Bahamas. Tropical storm force wind gusts were first observed along the coastal Broward County region by late on the afternoon of October 25th continuing into the evening. Several WeatherFlow sensors along and near the Broward County coast recorded Tropical Storm force wind gusts during the evening of October 25th with a peak wind gust of 63 mph observed at Port Everglades and 59 mph at Pompano Beach. However, as Hurricane Sandy continued to move slowly north and then northeast over the Atlantic waters north of the Bahamas through the 28th the main impact along the Broward County coast were large northeast swells generated by the storm which pummeled the Southeast Florida coast with severe beach erosion and coastal flooding. Large breaking waves of over 10 feet were estimated along the Broward County coast. As a result, major coastal flooding occurred along A1A in Fort Lauderdale Beach, mainly between Las Olas Boulevard and NE 20th Street. This resulted in this stretch of road being closed the entire weekend, along with a few others, as well as some neighborhoods being cut off. A few homes also sustained water damage. The large waves brought sea water and sand a couple of feet deep onto A1A with the inland extent being about 2 city blocks in some locales.||Nearest tide gauge at Virginia Key reported a maximum storm tide of 4.15 feet above mean lower low water (MLLW) on October 28th at 848 AM, along with a maximum storm surge of 1.44 feet. ||Only minor wind damage was noted, primarily to vegetation. A total 55,440 customers lost power. Exact damage figures are unknown for Broward County but are believed to be very high and related to the beach erosion and coastal flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure aloft was located off the Palm Beach County coast along with instability and tropical moisture across Southeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A school sign marquee got blown off during strong thunderstorm wind gusts at the Atlantic West Elementary School near the intersection of West Atlantic Boulevard and Rock Island Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure area off the North Florida coast and a broad upper trough moving into the eastern U.S. produced scattered afternoon thunderstorms across South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof of an outer building on the campus of Plantation High School was partially peeled off. Approximately 150 square feet of roof covering material was on the ground. Water entered the building, but no structural damage was noted to the steel joists. A few small trees were uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure aloft was located off the Palm Beach County coast along with instability and tropical moisture across Southeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter located in Margate reported two large trees down in her back yard from strong thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure aloft was located off the Palm Beach County coast along with instability and tropical moisture across Southeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two palm trees were downed by strong thunderstorm wind gusts in the western suburbs of Palm Beach County near the intersection of Northlake Boulevard and Ibis Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front was located across North Florida with an upper level trough moving into the eastern U.S. producing scattered thunderstorms over South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reports a downed palm tree on Kendall Drive between 122nd and 127th Avenues. Other reports and observations also indicated broken large tree branches in this same area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure area off the North Florida coast and a broad upper trough moving into the eastern U.S. produced scattered afternoon thunderstorms across South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported nickel-sized hail and a downed Ficus tree near the Palmetto Expressway/State Route 826 and LeJuene/Northwest 42nd Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure moved across North Florida. This interacted with abundant tropical moisture over Southeast Florida along with an unstable airmass to produce scattered afternoon thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported a rope-like funnel cloud over the Redlands west of Krome Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The outer bands from Tropical Storm Debby located in the Northeast Gulf of Mexico continued to move over South Florida. Severe thunderstorms developed during the late morning into the afternoon with severe wind gusts and eight tornadoes were also spawned. The eight tornadoes that occurred over a span of four hours was the most number of tornadoes in one day over the southern Florida peninsula since October 14, 1964 when Hurricane Isbell also spawned eight tornadoes. All of the tornadoes were of EF0 intensity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Golden Beach Police Department reported a waterspout moving onshore at the 400 block of Ocean Boulevard and quickly moving north along the beach side of Ocean Boulevard to the Broward County line. Path was approximately 0.5 miles. Beach chairs were tossed about 30 feet in the air, damage to trees and a hut in the 400-500 block of Ocean Boulevard. Most damage occurred at 625 Ocean Boulevard where doors and a large metal gate were blown down and twisted, along with tree branches snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface frontal boundary moved into South Florida during the afternoon and generated numerous to widespread severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening some with very large hail. Hail up to the size of baseballs and slightly larger was reported from two separate thunderstorms and this is only the sixth time since 1950 that baseball sized hail or larger has been reported anywhere in South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported in Loxahatchee. Time is approximate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Debby moved across the area from the northeast Gulf of Mexico. Deep tropical moisture combined with a stalled frontal boundary across north Florida over a period of several days caused extensive, flooding rainfall, as well as historic river flooding on the St. Marys River. A few severe storms developed each day, but the main impact was flooding rainfall and extensive river flooding which flooded homes in Baker, Charlton, Camden and Nassau counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding occurred across Starke. At 7 am on 6/25, emergency management reported several roads under water in the city including Bessent Road near Valley Road, Colley Road near the Hospital, NE 14th Ave, NE 17th Ave, SR 230 and at SR 16 and Old Lawtey Road. At 7:38 am, Alligator Creek over flowed with several homes threatened by flood waters. U.S. Highway 301 was closed from State Road 16 to Market Road due to flooding. Additional flooding concerns included North Water Street, East Mimosa Drive and Branford Drive in Starked. Flood control gates on Lake Sampson were opened due to high lake levels. There were 7 inches of rainfall during the past 24 hours across the county. At 8:47 am, a co-operative observer in Starked measured 6.5 inches of rainfall in the past 24 hours. The cost of damage was estimated, and it is likely greatly under-estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed in the residual moist and unstable airmass in the wake of Tropical Storm Isaac, producing both severe winds and a funnel cloud in central to northern Brevard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A SKYWARN spotter observed a funnel cloud just southwest of Merritt Island and southeast of Rockledge. The funnel was observed to be moving north, inland of US Highway 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaac passed southwest of east-central Florida, making its closest approach during the late afternoon and evening hours of August 26. Two tornadoes developed during the morning of August 27 and several tropical storm force wind gusts were observed. County-averaged rainfall amounts over the 2-day period were as high as 10-12 inches within Okeechobee, Martin, St. Lucie and Indian River Counties, with isolated totals of 14-17 inches. A majority of the heavy rain fell from early morning through late afternoon of August 27, when flooding resulted in numerous, temporary road closures. Water entered many homes in Okeechobee and Indian River Counties due to over-topping of local creeks/canals and overwhelming of drainage systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent heavy rainbands from Tropical Storm Isaac produced areas of urban and lowland flooding across portions of the county. Rainfall from the morning of August 26 until the evening of August 27 averaged 3 to 6 inches, with isolated higher totals, most of which fell during the morning and afternoon of August 27. The most |significant impacts occurred in St. Cloud and rural locations farther south and east. Several roads were temporarily closed due to standing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaac passed southwest of east-central Florida, making its closest approach during the late afternoon and evening hours of August 26. Two tornadoes developed during the morning of August 27 and several tropical storm force wind gusts were observed. County-averaged rainfall amounts over the 2-day period were as high as 10-12 inches within Okeechobee, Martin, St. Lucie and Indian River Counties, with isolated totals of 14-17 inches. A majority of the heavy rain fell from early morning through late afternoon of August 27, when flooding resulted in numerous, temporary road closures. Water entered many homes in Okeechobee and Indian River Counties due to over-topping of local creeks/canals and overwhelming of drainage systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent heavy rainbands from Tropical Storm Isaac produced widespread flooding across the county. Rainfall totals from the morning of August 26 through late on August 27 averaged 5 to 10 inches, with isolated totals of 12 to 14 inches. Peak wind gusts reached 35-45 mph along the coast and shore of Lake Okeechobee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical airmass and a weak disturbance combined to produce heavy rainfall across portions of northwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24 hour rainfall total of 3.79 inches was recorded by APRSWXNET site C8103 located on Saint George Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level trough of low pressure covered the eastern U.S. with ample tropical moisture across South Florida and an unstable air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a wind gust estimated at 60 mph. A railroad crossing arm was snapped along State Road 710 6 miles west northwest of Riviera Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough of low pressure over the eastern U.S. produced severe thunderstorms with high wind gusts and large hail during the morning over Miami-Dade County. Reports were received of numerous trees downed between 8 and 9 AM EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported quarter-sized hail in Leisure City near the intersection of SW 288th Street and the Florida Turnpike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough of low pressure over the eastern U.S. produced severe thunderstorms with high wind gusts and large hail during the morning over Miami-Dade County. Reports were received of numerous trees downed between 8 and 9 AM EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported wind gusts of 60 mph near the intersection of US 1 and SW 248th Street in Princeton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm moved east across Vero Beach and produced local wind damage at the county jail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm uprooted a large tree at the Indian River County jail. The ground was very moist from flooding a week earlier and likely contributed to the uprooting. A fence was damaged by the falling tree. Also at the jail, two law enforcement vehicles experienced minor damage after being impacted by debris. One vehicle was hit by a plastic chair and the other one was damaged when a wheeled storage trailer rolled into it. Measured wind gusts at the nearby Vero Beach airport reached 52 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Debby moved across the area from the northeast Gulf of Mexico. Deep tropical moisture combined with a stalled frontal boundary across north Florida over a period of several days caused extensive, flooding rainfall, as well as historic river flooding on the St. Marys River. A few severe storms developed each day, but the main impact was flooding rainfall and extensive river flooding which flooded homes in Baker, Charlton, Camden and Nassau counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record flooding occurred in Baker county with a historic crest on the St. Marys River due to persistent heavy rainfall over NE Florida and SE Georgia in the river's upstream charge basin. Radar estimated that 15 to 25 inches fell in the charge basin over several days. On 6/25 at 9:32 pm, a spotter reported 3.46 inches of rainfall in the past 5 hours at a location 3 miles ESE of Sanderson. Ditches were at capacity in the area at the time of the report. At 10:25 pm that evening, the media reported 4.08 inches of rainfall since 5 pm that afternoon in Glen St. Mary. Water was crossing roads west of Glen St. Mary. At 10:30 pm, the media reported Interstate 10 eastbound at mile marker 317 near Sanderson was closed due to flooding. At 10:44 pm, Interstate 10 westbound at mile marker 324 near Glen St. Mary was closed due to flooding per a media report. Flooding continued through the end of the month. On 6/28 significant river flooding ensured on the St. Marys with numerous mobile homes flooded and a total loss. Other homes were also flooded. At 10:50 am, baker county emergency management reported a bridge floating down the St. Marys River.  At 1:06 pm, the State Road 121 bridge was flooded by the St. Marys River and was closed. The County Road 127 bridge over Cedar Creek collapsed. The cost of property loss was estimated and is likely greatly under-estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Debby moved across the area from the northeast Gulf of Mexico. Deep tropical moisture combined with a stalled frontal boundary across north Florida over a period of several days caused extensive, flooding rainfall, as well as historic river flooding on the St. Marys River. A few severe storms developed each day, but the main impact was flooding rainfall and extensive river flooding which flooded homes in Baker, Charlton, Camden and Nassau counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 7:26 am the park service reported a 24-hour rainfall total of 4.1 inches about 2 miles east of Lake City. At 9 am on 6/25, law enforcement reported eastbound Interstate 10 at mile marker 301 was closed due to high flood waters. At 9:15 am that morning, law enforcement reported U.S. Highway 41 southbound was closed near Interstate 10 due to flood waters. At 9 pm, law enforcement reported Interstate 10 eastbound at mile marker 301 was closed due to standing flood waters. At 8:16 am on 6/26, a co-operative observer reported a 24 hour rainfall total of 8.77 inches in Lake City. On 6/28 at 3:57 pm, the public reported a monthly rainfall total of 27.55 inches with 19.99 inches between the 23rd-26th about 5 miles northeast of Ichetucknee Springs.  The cost of damage was estimated, and is likely greatly under estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Air Force Wind Tower 714 at the Kennedy Space Center recorded a wind gust of 57 kts/66 mph during a severe thunderstorm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS employee estimated wind gusts of 60 mph in Princeton at the intersection of SW 259th Street and SW 125th Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the most part, Hurricane Isaac missed the eastern Panhandle and Big Bend of Florida.  However, the outer remnants of Hurricane Isaac spawned a couple of funnel clouds and tornadoes, and some coastal flooding did occur across portions of Wakulla, Franklin, Gulf, and Bay counties in association with the storm surge.  ||Significant erosion occurred across Franklin county with estimates of around $500K in damage from erosion from a WCTV news article.  The storm surge was measured at 3.45 feet at Apalachicola at 1200 UTC on the 28th with a total storm tide of 3.92 inches.  The storm surge was measured at 2.50 feet at Panama City at 1400 UTC on the 28th with a total storm tide of 3.50 feet.  Also in Franklin county, Water Street in Apalachicola flood with over one foot of water in some places.  Some flooding occurred in Saint George Island State Park with water passing the dune line and flooding parking areas.  Surf heights were in excess of 6 to 8 feet.||In Wakulla county, minor coastal flooding was reported in typical flood prone areas around Saint Marks.  Two to four inches of water was reported inside a few businesses in Saint Marks.  One vacant home experienced minor water intrusion around the time of highest tide in Shell Point.  ||In Gulf county, Indian Pass Road flooded around the time of high tide.  Saint Joseph State Park was closed due to water inundating the access road.||In Bay County, there was minor inundation of a few roads in downtown Panama City near 7th Avenue.  Water depth ranged between a few inches to one foot in places.  Flooding of roads was also reported in areas of Lynn Haven closer to West Bay.  Very high surf resulted in moderate beach erosion with surf heights in excess of 8 to 10 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public sighted a funnel cloud at Baily Bay Bridge on Highway 77 near Marina Bay Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Debby moved across the area from the northeast Gulf of Mexico. Deep tropical moisture combined with a stalled frontal boundary across north Florida over a period of several days caused extensive, flooding rainfall, as well as historic river flooding on the St. Marys River. A few severe storms developed each day, but the main impact was flooding rainfall and extensive river flooding which flooded homes in Baker, Charlton, Camden and Nassau counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding occurred across Jacksonville. On 6/25 at 3:17 am, an NWS employee reported minor street flooding along Shindler Drive between 103rd Street and Collins Road in Jacksonville. At 7:11 am, the public reported 8 inches of water in a home at 6314 105th Street in the Normandy area of Jacksonville. At 8:04 am, an NWS employee reported 7.4 inches of rainfall in Arlington, about 6 miles west of Jacksonville Beach. At 10 am, Duval county emergency management reported flooding at a home in the Boone Park area of Riverside. There were about 40 road closures at the time across Duval county due to flooding. At 9:09 pm, a spotter reported flooding in the Country Roads Mobile Home Park about 4 miles NW of Orange Park. On 6/26 at 8:11 am, a spotter reported 4.25 inches of rainfall in the past 12 hours. There was about 9 inches of storm total rainfall with minor street flooding. At 3:53 pm, a NWS employee reported the intersection of 103rd Street and Shindler Drive was closed due to high flood water. At 5:45 pm, the public reported a creek was overflowing into home on Old Plank Road. The water depth was about 18 inches. At 6 pm, the public reported road and yard flooding along Blackthorn Road. At 7:02 pm, 12 to 18 inches of water was over the road about 3 mile WSW of Ortega due to a nearby creek out of its banks. At 8:30 pm on the 26th, a retention pond overflowed and water was approaching a home on Collins Ridge Blvd. On 9/27 at 6:34 am, Duval County Emergency Management reported the intersection of Garden Street and Imeson Road was flooded with at least one foot of standing water.  The cost of damage was estimated, and it is likely greatly under-estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure area over the western Caribbean Sea combined with deep tropical moisture associated with a tropical wave produced periods of heavy rainfall across South Florida. The combination of the heavy rains and gusty winds led to about 2000 people losing power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof of an apartment building in Lauderhill partially collapsed due to heavy rains, causing the relocation of residents in six apartments.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaac passed southwest of east-central Florida, making its closest approach during the late afternoon and evening hours of August 26. Two tornadoes developed during the morning of August 27 and several tropical storm force wind gusts were observed. County-averaged rainfall amounts over the 2-day period were as high as 10-12 inches within Okeechobee, Martin, St. Lucie and Indian River Counties, with isolated totals of 14-17 inches. A majority of the heavy rain fell from early morning through late afternoon of August 27, when flooding resulted in numerous, temporary road closures. Water entered many homes in Okeechobee and Indian River Counties due to over-topping of local creeks/canals and overwhelming of drainage systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down just southeast of Indian River County Fire Station number 7 on 12th Street in Vero Beach. A discontinuous path of damage stretched 1.6 miles through the Vero Beach Estates and Countryside Village Mobile Home Parks, crossed State Road 60, and lifted just north of the Paradise Park residential area. A total of 95 structures (mostly mobile homes) were damaged, with 62 receiving minor damage and 33 sustaining major damage due to wind and rain. There were several short locations along the path were damage was minimal or non-evident. DI2 DOD4 LB; DI3 DOD4 LB-EXP, DI4 DOD7 LB.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface frontal boundary moved into South Florida during the afternoon and generated numerous to widespread severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening some with very large hail. Hail up to the size of baseballs and slightly larger was reported from two separate thunderstorms and this is only the sixth time since 1950 that baseball sized hail or larger has been reported anywhere in South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trained spotters reported quarter sized hail in Boca Raton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary from pre-existing thunderstorms intersected a storm developing along the east coast sea breeze. This caused enough turning in the lower atmosphere to produce a long-lived funnel cloud just north of the Pineda Causeway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple observers spotted a funnel cloud as it developed west of US Highway 1 in Rockledge and continued east-northeast over the Indian River and south Merritt Island without touching down over land or water. Numerous photos, as well as a video documenting the entire event were provided to the NWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level low pressure area moved across Central Florida and combined with deep tropical moisture over South Florida producing scattered afternoon and early evening thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported multiple trees downed across the road near the intersection of Wellington Trace Road and Forest Hill Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant tropical moisture along with instability and very light winds allowed for scattered showers to develop along the east coast sea breeze.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Law Enforcement Officer along with the Homestead Airport Observer reported a tiny funnel cloud developing at about 2 miles southwest from the Homestead General Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level low pressure area over Western Cuba caused tropical moisture and instability to flow across South Florida. This produced scattered afternoon and early evening thunderstorms across portions of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Glades County Emergency Manager reported one inch hail on Wayman Road near U.S. 27 west of Moore Haven.

A third 29-year old male was taken to Homestead General Hospital where he was treated for minor injuries and released.

Tropical Storm Isaac passed southwest of east-central Florida, making its closest approach during the late afternoon and evening hours of August 26. Two tornadoes developed during the morning of August 27 and several tropical storm force wind gusts were observed. County-averaged rainfall amounts over the 2-day period were as high as 10-12 inches within Okeechobee, Martin, St. Lucie and Indian River Counties, with isolated totals of 14-17 inches. A majority of the heavy rain fell from early morning through late afternoon of August 27, when flooding resulted in numerous, temporary road closures. Water entered many homes in Okeechobee and Indian River Counties due to over-topping of local creeks/canals and overwhelming of drainage systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent heavy rainbands from Tropical Storm Isaac produced widespread urban and lowland flooding across much of the county. Rainfall from the morning of August 26 until the evening of August 27 averaged 5 to 10 inches, with isolated totals of 12 to 14 inches, most of which fell during the morning and afternoon of August 27. The most significant impacts occurred near the coast, from Lakewood Park to Fort Pierce, White City, and Port St. Lucie. Numerous roads were temporarily closed due to standing water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent heavy rainbands from Tropical Storm Isaac produced widespread urban and lowland flooding across much of the county. Rainfall from the morning of August 26 until the evening of August 27 averaged 4 to 8 inches, with isolated totals of 14 to 17 inches, most of which fell during the morning and afternoon of August 27. The most significant impacts occurred near the coast from Winter Beach to Vero Beach and Florida Ridge. Twenty seven manufactured homes and two single family homes were damaged by water intrusion and 20 roads were temporarily closed due to standing water during the height of the flooding.

The most significant impacts occurred within the City of Okeechobee and adjacent rural locations, including Taylor Creek.  Fourteen homes experienced major damage and another 146 had minor water intrusion (from either leaking roofs and/or high levels of standing water, mainly from over-topping local creek/canals. Several roads were temporarily closed due to standing water.

However, the outer remnants of Hurricane Isaac spawned a couple of funnel clouds and tornadoes, and some coastal flooding did occur across portions of Wakulla, Franklin, Gulf, and Bay counties in association with the storm surge.  ||Significant erosion occurred across Franklin county with estimates of around $500K in damage from erosion from a WCTV news article.  The storm surge was measured at 3.45 feet at Apalachicola at 1200 UTC on the 28th with a total storm tide of 3.92 inches.  The storm surge was measured at 2.50 feet at Panama City at 1400 UTC on the 28th with a total storm tide of 3.50 feet.  Also in Franklin county, Water Street in Apalachicola flood with over one foot of water in some places.  Some flooding occurred in Saint George Island State Park with water passing the dune line and flooding parking areas.  Surf heights were in excess of 6 to 8 feet.||In Wakulla county, minor coastal flooding was reported in typical flood prone areas around Saint Marks.  Two to four inches of water was reported inside a few businesses in Saint Marks.  One vacant home experienced minor water intrusion around the time of highest tide in Shell Point.  ||In Gulf county, Indian Pass Road flooded around the time of high tide.  Saint Joseph State Park was closed due to water inundating the access road.||In Bay County, there was minor inundation of a few roads in downtown Panama City near 7th Avenue.  Water depth ranged between a few inches to one foot in places.  Flooding of roads was also reported in areas of Lynn Haven closer to West Bay.  Very high surf resulted in moderate beach erosion with surf heights in excess of 8 to 10 feet.

Heavy rain developed over the area during the morning and continued into the afternoon. The heaviest rain fell across southern Escambia county. Storm total rainfall amounts were between 10 to 20 inches. This caused widespread flooding in many areas especially around Pensacola.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused numerous roads and businesses to be flooded in the Pensacola area including downtown Pensacola around Palafox. Pensacola Regional Airport received 11.12 inches of rain since midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaac passed southwest of east-central Florida, making its closest approach during the late afternoon and evening hours of August 26. Two tornadoes developed during the morning of August 27 and several tropical storm force wind gusts were observed. County-averaged rainfall amounts over the 2-day period were as high as 10-12 inches within Okeechobee, Martin, St. Lucie and Indian River Counties, with isolated totals of 14-17 inches. A majority of the heavy rain fell from early morning through late afternoon of August 27, when flooding resulted in numerous, temporary road closures. Water entered many homes in Okeechobee and Indian River Counties due to over-topping of local creeks/canals and overwhelming of drainage systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent heavy rainbands from Tropical Storm Isaac produced areas of flooding across portions of the county. Rainfall totals from the morning of August 26 through late on August 27 averaged 3 to 6 inches, with isolated higher totals. An EF-0 tornado occurred near St. Cloud and damaged several structures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The outer bands from Tropical Storm Debby located in the northeast Gulf of Mexico spawned two tornadoes in the Naples area both with EF0 intensity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touchdown occurred at the North Collier Hospital in North Naples on the corner of Immokalee and Goodlette-Frank Roads. Damage was reported to trees and light poles broken off, but no structural damage to the hospital. One person was struck by a downed tree limb and was treated on-site. The tornado was likely spawned by the same storm that produced the earlier tornado in East Naples.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough crossed east-central Florida and increased instability. This combined with abundant moisture and boundary collisions produced severe storms and areas of damage across Brevard County, as well isolated wind damage near Apopka and Okeechobee. Several funnel clouds and small hail were also observed during the episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large plate glass window was blown in on Banana River Boulevard in Cocoa Beach as a severe thunderstorm moved through. A screen was also damaged.

Rainfall totals from the morning of August 26 through late on August 27 averaged 4 to 8 inches, with isolated totals of 14 to 17 inches. Peak wind gusts reached 35-45 mph along the coast. An EF-0 tornado occurred in Vero Beach and damaged 95 structures (mostly mobile homes).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along the seabreeze front and caused thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 10 trees and numerous branches were downed in the Saint Ives Boulevard and East Linden Drive area.  The wind also damaged fences, basketball hoops, and pool screens.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving Tropical Storm Debby created significant impacts to the area for several days, including freshwater flooding, storm surge flooding, and trees and power lines blown down due to gusty winds.  ||In Wakulla county, significant inland flooding occurred from very heavy rainfall.  Rainfall caused numerous small creeks, streams, and rivers to rapidly exceed their banks and flood adjacent communities.  The Sopchoppy River crested at 36.8 feet on Tuesday, June 26th.  This is the flood of record for the Sopchoppy.  Numerous homes were impacted by flood waters, some up to the second story.  Two bridges were damaged by flood waters, and numerous roads around the county were washed out or closed during the peak of the storm.  Wakulla county also experience moderate storm surge flooding on June 25th around the time of high tide.  Saint Marks had numerous roads underwater with several area businesses affected by high water.  Water levels were estimated between one to one and a half feet above ground level.  The storm surge at Saint Marks was estimated at 4.00 feet with a total storm tide of 6.50 feet on June 25th at 6:00 pm EDT.  Additional flooding occurred near Panacea.  U.S. Highway 98 was over-washed just north of town.  Mandatory evacuations were ordered south of US-98 and around the Sopchoppy River.  In total, there were 17 water rescues rescuing a total of 67 people.      ||In Leon county, several roads in the southern part of the county were washed out or closed to due flood waters.  ||In Franklin county, several roads across the northern portion of the county were flooded due to heavy rainfall.  Moderate storm surge flooding occurred around Alligator Point with Alligator Point Road being damaged or washed out in a few places.  Evacuations were ordered for Saint George Island, Alligator Point, and areas along the Carrabelle River.  Nine people had to be rescued by the U.S. Coast Guard on Dog Island when they were trapped in a home during the storm and surrounded by water.  The storm surge measured by the gauge at Apalachicola was 3.51 feet with a total storm tide of 4.31 feet on June 25th at 2:24 pm CDT.    ||In Gulf county, water level rise around high tide forced the closure and eventual washout of Indian Pass Road.  ||In Jefferson county, a few roads flooded due to heavy rainfall on the 26th.  The most notable was that Highway 27 west of Highway 19 was closed for a brief time due to flooding.  ||In Madison county, water from a retention pond surrounded a residence near the city of Madison.  ||In Dixie county, several roads north of Cross City experienced flooding.  River flooding along the Steinhatchee River impacted at least 40 homes.  Moderate storm surge flooding occurred in Horseshoe Beach to several homes near the coast.  Water up to one and a half feet high surrounded several outbuildings.  The storm surge was estimated at 4.00 feet at Horseshoe Beach with a storm tide of 7.00 feet on June 25th at 6:00 pm EDT.   ||In Lafayette county, Highway 51 South flooded on the 27th.  The Steinhatchee River nearly over-topped the bridge over Highway 51 in the southern portion of the county.||In Bay county, moderate beach erosion occurred with a storm surge value at Panama City measured at 1.50 feet with a storm tide of 2.42 feet on June 25th at 10:06 am CDT.

Tropical Storm Isaac tracked WNW over the Florida Straits and over the southeast and east-central Gulf of Mexico. Fringe affects on the storm's NE side were experienced over Flagler county where periods of heavy rainfall occurred in squalls. Gusty winds of 40 to 50 mph were also reported in squalls which felled isolated trees across northeast Florida. A few trees were blown down on the 26th, one in Alachua county around 10 pm across south Main Street in Gainesville. A few other were blown down across Flagler County on the 26th and 27th. No major damage was reported associated with fringe affects from T.S. Isaac.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall amounts across Flagler County were gathered over a 3 day period from Aug. 26th through August 28th. Palm Coast communities measured 2 to 3.25 inches of rainfall with wind gusts of 40 mph. Some minor flooding occurred in parking lots. Farther south near I-95 in the southern part of the county rainfall amounts were near 2.25 inches and a tree was blown down on Seaman Path. Flagler Beach measured 1.5 inches. Bunnell measured 2.49 inches. A few trees were blown down in the western part of the county, one over Walnut and Forest Park near Daytona North. Another tree was down on Shell Bluff Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level short wave trough moved over the forecast area during the day. This feature combined with a dominant west coast sea breeze over a moist and unstable airmass fueled scattered strong storms with locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts within 20 minutes were 0.50 inches to 0.74 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level short wave trough moved over the forecast area during the day. This feature combined with a dominant west coast sea breeze over a moist and unstable airmass fueled scattered strong storms with locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch of rainfall fell in 20 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roofs of several carports were blown down at the Malabar Lakes Apartments.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado, with winds estimated near 65 mph, touched down in rural St. Cloud and destroyed an old, abandoned mobile home (floor attachment to the frame was severely rotted), then continued across a wooded lot and open, old citrus groves, causing minor-moderate shingle damage to a block home (house window was broken from debris impact), and minor shrub and fence damage within the yard of a neaby home. Insulation and small debris from the mobile home was carried downstream throughout the distance of the track. DI3 DOD6 Below LB; DI2 DOD1 EXP; DI27 DOD1 EXP.

Wakulla county also experience moderate storm surge flooding on June 25th around the time of high tide.  Saint Marks had numerous roads underwater with several area businesses affected by high water.  Water levels were estimated between one to one and a half feet above ground level.  The storm surge at Saint Marks was estimated at 4.00 feet with a total storm tide of 6.50 feet on June 25th at 6:00 pm EDT.  Additional flooding occurred near Panacea.  U.S. Highway 98 was over-washed just north of town.  Mandatory evacuations were ordered south of US-98 and around the Sopchoppy River.  In total, there were 17 water rescues rescuing a total of 67 people.      ||In Leon county, several roads in the southern part of the county were washed out or closed to due flood waters.  ||In Franklin county, several roads across the northern portion of the county were flooded due to heavy rainfall.  Moderate storm surge flooding occurred around Alligator Point with Alligator Point Road being damaged or washed out in a few places.  Evacuations were ordered for Saint George Island, Alligator Point, and areas along the Carrabelle River.  Nine people had to be rescued by the U.S. Coast Guard on Dog Island when they were trapped in a home during the storm and surrounded by water.  The storm surge measured by the gauge at Apalachicola was 3.51 feet with a total storm tide of 4.31 feet on June 25th at 2:24 pm CDT.    ||In Gulf county, water level rise around high tide forced the closure and eventual washout of Indian Pass Road.  ||In Jefferson county, a few roads flooded due to heavy rainfall on the 26th.  The most notable was that Highway 27 west of Highway 19 was closed for a brief time due to flooding.  ||In Madison county, water from a retention pond surrounded a residence near the city of Madison.  ||In Dixie county, several roads north of Cross City experienced flooding.  River flooding along the Steinhatchee River impacted at least 40 homes.  Moderate storm surge flooding occurred in Horseshoe Beach to several homes near the coast.  Water up to one and a half feet high surrounded several outbuildings.  The storm surge was estimated at 4.00 feet at Horseshoe Beach with a storm tide of 7.00 feet on June 25th at 6:00 pm EDT.   ||In Lafayette county, Highway 51 South flooded on the 27th.  The Steinhatchee River nearly over-topped the bridge over Highway 51 in the southern portion of the county.||In Bay county, moderate beach erosion occurred with a storm surge value at Panama City measured at 1.50 feet with a storm tide of 2.42 feet on June 25th at 10:06 am CDT.

A mid level short wave trough moved over the forecast area during the day. This feature combined with a dominant west coast sea breeze over a moist and unstable airmass fueled scattered strong storms with locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two men working in a bucket truck were struck by lightning at the 8800 block of Moncrief Dinsmore Road around 1 pm. One man was in the truck at the time. Both men were taken to the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough crossed east-central Florida and increased instability. This combined with abundant moisture and boundary collisions produced severe storms and areas of damage across Brevard County, as well isolated wind damage near Apopka and Okeechobee. Several funnel clouds and small hail were also observed during the episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wooden power poles to the northeast of Okeechobee, near 7200 U.S. Highway 441 North, were tilted by severe thunderstorm winds, with the two poles experiencing broken cross members. While a funnel cloud was reported in the area (see separate event), the wind damage appeared to be due to straight line winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Okeechobee County Fire/Rescue relayed a public report of a funnel cloud near Okeechobee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The outer bands from Tropical Storm Debby located in the northeast Gulf of Mexico spawned two tornadoes in the Naples area both with EF0 intensity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was first observed as a waterspout just of Isle of Capri and then tracked into the Naples area. Survey of damage revealed mostly broken tree branches, one which fell on and damaged a car, uprooted small trees and a palm tree split in half. A pool lanai was heavily damaged, but only minor roof damage was noted. Damage path indicated a low-end EF0 tornado with winds likely no more than 70-75 mph. Path length was discontinuous approximately 1.7 miles with width likely no more than 20 yards.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred across southeastern Leon county in association with Tropical Storm Debby.  The most significant flooding occurred around the Woodville area.  Reports included over a foot of water on Amherst Road in the Forest Grove subdivision.  Jeremy Road was also impossible with water up to the hood of a truck.  Shumard Drive at Canyon Creek Road was washed out.  Rhoades Cemetary Road was closed from Woodville Highway eastward, and Wild Cherry Road was also closed due to flooding.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Catastrophic flooding occurred in Wakulla county as a result of Tropical Storm Debby, especially near Sopchoppy where the Sopchoppy River crested above its previous record flood stage.  Over 27 inches of rain was measured in the area.  Over 400 structures were affected by flooding, including over 170 mobile homes and nearly 200 single family residences.  Of these structures, 40 were deemed totally destroyed, 61 had major flood damage, 41 had minor flood damage, and 271 were affected by flooding.  The Wakulla county property appraiser estimated damage at $8,785,087.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The outer bands from Tropical Storm Debby located in the Northeast Gulf of Mexico continued to move over South Florida. Severe thunderstorms developed during the late morning into the afternoon with severe wind gusts and eight tornadoes were also spawned. The eight tornadoes that occurred over a span of four hours was the most number of tornadoes in one day over the southern Florida peninsula since October 14, 1964 when Hurricane Isbell also spawned eight tornadoes. All of the tornadoes were of EF0 intensity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Glades County Sheriffs Office reported a brief tornado touchdown near Pine Avenue in the community of Muse. A member of the public reported that a barn roof was torn off.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm spotter videos, observations and Doppler radar data indicated that a waterspout moving NNE over Sullivan Bay passed about 3 miles east of Cape Romano then moved over Blackwater Bay and onto the mainland three miles east of Goodland. The tornado was captured on video crossing US 41 near a roadside picnic area about 1/2 mile east of the Collier-Seminole State Park entrance. Damage was confined to trees in a non-populated area of the Everglades.

Rainfall caused numerous small creeks, streams, and rivers to rapidly exceed their banks and flood adjacent communities.  The Sopchoppy River crested at 36.8 feet on Tuesday, June 26th.  This is the flood of record for the Sopchoppy.  Numerous homes were impacted by flood waters, some up to the second story.  Two bridges were damaged by flood waters, and numerous roads around the county were washed out or closed during the peak of the storm.  Wakulla county also experience moderate storm surge flooding on June 25th around the time of high tide.  Saint Marks had numerous roads underwater with several area businesses affected by high water.  Water levels were estimated between one to one and a half feet above ground level.  The storm surge at Saint Marks was estimated at 4.00 feet with a total storm tide of 6.50 feet on June 25th at 6:00 pm EDT.  Additional flooding occurred near Panacea.  U.S. Highway 98 was over-washed just north of town.  Mandatory evacuations were ordered south of US-98 and around the Sopchoppy River.  In total, there were 17 water rescues rescuing a total of 67 people.      ||In Leon county, several roads in the southern part of the county were washed out or closed to due flood waters.  ||In Franklin county, several roads across the northern portion of the county were flooded due to heavy rainfall.  Moderate storm surge flooding occurred around Alligator Point with Alligator Point Road being damaged or washed out in a few places.  Evacuations were ordered for Saint George Island, Alligator Point, and areas along the Carrabelle River.  Nine people had to be rescued by the U.S. Coast Guard on Dog Island when they were trapped in a home during the storm and surrounded by water.  The storm surge measured by the gauge at Apalachicola was 3.51 feet with a total storm tide of 4.31 feet on June 25th at 2:24 pm CDT.    ||In Gulf county, water level rise around high tide forced the closure and eventual washout of Indian Pass Road.  ||In Jefferson county, a few roads flooded due to heavy rainfall on the 26th.  The most notable was that Highway 27 west of Highway 19 was closed for a brief time due to flooding.  ||In Madison county, water from a retention pond surrounded a residence near the city of Madison.  ||In Dixie county, several roads north of Cross City experienced flooding.  River flooding along the Steinhatchee River impacted at least 40 homes.  Moderate storm surge flooding occurred in Horseshoe Beach to several homes near the coast.  Water up to one and a half feet high surrounded several outbuildings.  The storm surge was estimated at 4.00 feet at Horseshoe Beach with a storm tide of 7.00 feet on June 25th at 6:00 pm EDT.   ||In Lafayette county, Highway 51 South flooded on the 27th.  The Steinhatchee River nearly over-topped the bridge over Highway 51 in the southern portion of the county.||In Bay county, moderate beach erosion occurred with a storm surge value at Panama City measured at 1.50 feet with a storm tide of 2.42 feet on June 25th at 10:06 am CDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total rainfall of 12.62 inches fell during Tropical Storm Debby.
